blocks:
Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (٢)
The [gradual] sending down of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-knowing, (2)
غَافِرِ ٱلذَّنۢبِ وَقَابِلِ ٱلتَّوْبِ شَدِيدِ ٱلْعِقَابِ ذِى ٱلطَّوْلِ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ إِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٣)
forgiver of sins and acceptor of repentance, severe in retribution, [yet] all-bountiful; there is no god except Him, [and] toward Him is the destination. (3)
مَا يُجَـٰدِلُ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَلَا يَغْرُرْكَ تَقَلُّبُهُمْ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ (٤)
No one disputes the signs of Allah except the faithless. So do not be misled by their bustle in the towns. (4)
كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۖ وَهَمَّتْ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍۭ بِرَسُولِهِمْ لِيَأْخُذُوهُ ۖ وَجَـٰدَلُوا۟ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا۟ بِهِ ٱلْحَقَّ فَأَخَذْتُهُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عِقَابِ (٥)
The people of Noah denied before them and the [heathen] factions [who came] after them. Every nation attempted to lay hands on their apostle, and disputed erroneously to refute the truth. Then I seized them; so how was My retribution?! (5)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ (٦)
That is how the word of your Lord became due concerning the faithless, that they shall be inmates of the Fire. (6)

(Commentary)

The Surah discusses the arrogance (الاستكبار) of the disbelievers and their disputation (المجادلة) through falsehood to refute the truth to which they are invited. For this reason, we see it mentioning their disputation and returning to it time and again: مَا يُجَادِلُ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَلَا يَغْرُرْكَ تَقَلُّبُهُمْ فِي الْبِلَادِ "None dispute the signs of Allah except the faithless. So do not be deluded by their turnover in the land" (40:4); الَّذِينَ يُجَادِلُونَ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَانٍ أَتَاهُمْ ۖ كَبُرَ مَقْتًا "Those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that may have come to them—[that is] greatly outrageous" (40:35); أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يُجَادِلُونَ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ أَنَّىٰ يُصْرَفُونَ "Have you not regarded those who dispute the signs of Allah, how they are drawn away?" (40:69).

Thus, the Surah breaks their arrogance and disputation by mentioning how Allah punished the past nations who denied the truth, and the humiliating punishment He has prepared for them, by mentioning a portion of what will befall them in the Hereafter.

It refutes the falsehood of their claims through various arguments that articulate His oneness in Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية). It commands the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to be patient and promises him and the believers victory. It also commands them to notify the disbelievers that he is submitted to his Lord and will never abandon His worship, so they should despair of him.

The Surah is entirely Meccan due to the interconnectedness of its verses and the testimony of its contents to that effect. Any claim that certain verses were revealed in Medina is not to be heeded, and this will be pointed out later, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: حم تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتَابِ مِنَ اللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ "Ha, Meem. The sent-down [revelation] of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-knowing" (40:1-2). "The sending down" (التنزيل) is a verbal noun used in the sense of the object (المفعول). Thus, His saying: "the sending down of the Book" (تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتَابِ) is an instance of appending an attribute to its subject; the implied meaning is: "This is a Book sent down from Allah."

The specific mention of the two attributes, "the All-mighty" (الْعَزِيزِ) and "the All-knowing" (الْعَلِيمِ), is said to be an allusion to the miraculous nature (الإعجاز) of the Quran and the various types of knowledge it contains, which exceed the scope of human understanding. It is also said to be for the sake of rhetorical variety (التفنن).

The correct view is to say: Since the Surah discusses the denial of the deniers and their disputation regarding the signs of Allah through falsehood out of ignorance—while they consider it knowledge and take pride in it, as He narrates about them at the end of the Surah: فَلَمَّا جَاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ فَرِحُوا بِمَا عِنْدَهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ "When their apostles brought them manifest proofs, they exulted in the knowledge they possessed" (40:83); and as He narrates Pharaoh’s words to his people regarding Moses: إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَنْ يُبَدِّلَ دِينَكُمْ أَوْ أَنْ يُظْهِرَ فِي الْأَرْضِ الْفَسَادَ "I fear that he will change your religion, or cause corruption to appear in the land" (40:26), and his saying to them: مَا أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا مَا أَرَىٰ وَمَا أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ الرَّشَادِ "I only show you what I see [to be right], and I only guide you to the way of rectitude" (40:29)—

He opened the discourse in the Surah with that which alludes to the fact that this Book revealed to them is a revelation from One who is All-mighty (عزيز) in an absolute sense. No overcomer can defeat Him such that He would fear their dominance or arrogance—based on their delusions—over what He has revealed. He is All-knowing (عليم) in an absolute sense; no ignorance or error enters His knowledge. Therefore, their disputation through falsehood cannot resist the truth He has revealed and clarified with His dazzling proofs.

This view is supported by what follows in the next verse: غَافِرِ الذَّنْبِ وَقَابِلِ التَّوْبِ "Forgiver of sin and Accepter of repentance" etc., as we shall explain.

His saying, the Exalted: غَافِرِ الذَّنْبِ وَقَابِلِ التَّوْبِ شَدِيدِ الْعِقَابِ ذِي الطَّوْلِ ۖ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ "Forgiver of sin and Accepter of repentance, severe in retribution, and possessor of bounty. There is no god except Him, and to Him is the return" (40:3). The use of the active participle (اسم الفاعل) form in "Forgiver of sin" (غَافِرِ الذَّنْبِ) and "Accepter of repentance" (قَابِلِ التَّوْبِ) is—perhaps—to indicate continuous renewal (الاستمرار التجددي). For forgiveness and the acceptance of repentance are among His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية); He, the Exalted, continues to forgive sin after sin and accept repentance after repentance.

"Accepter of repentance" was conjoined to what preceded it, rather than to "severe in retribution" (شَدِيدِ الْعِقَابِ) or "possessor of bounty" (ذِي الطَّوْلِ), because "Forgiver of sin" and "Accepter of repentance" together constitute a single attribute related to sinful servants; He forgives them sometimes through repentance and sometimes through other means, such as intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah.

Retribution (العقاب) and punishing (المعاقبة) refer to the reckoning that occurs as a consequence (عاقبة) of sin. Al-Raghib said: "The end (العقب) and the outcome (العقبى) are specific to reward, such as: خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا 'better in respect of reward and better in respect of outcome' (18:44). And the Exalted said: أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمْ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ 'For such there will be the ultimate abode' (13:22). As for the consequence (العاقبة), its general use is specific to reward, such as: وَالْعَاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ 'and the outcome will be in favor of the God-conscious' (28:83). However, when used in a possessive construction, it may be used for punishment, such as: ثُمَّ كَانَ عَاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا 'Then the fate of those who committed evil was...' (30:10). His saying: فَكَانَ عَاقِبَتَهُمَا أَنَّهُمَا فِي النَّارِ 'So the outcome for both of them was that they will be in the Fire' (59:17) could be a metaphorical use derived from its opposite. As for punishment (العقوبة), penalizing (المعاقبة), and retribution (العقاب), they are specific to torment." [End of quote].

Thus, "Severe in Retribution" (شديد العقاب), like "Possessor of Vengeance" (ذو انتقام), is among the Beautiful Names of Allah (أسماء الله الحسنى) that describe His attribute in the aspect of torment, just as "the All-Forgiving" (الغفور) and "the Most Merciful" (الرحيم) describe His attribute in the aspect of mercy.

Bounty (الطول)—according to al-Majma‘—is a favor whose duration is extended for its recipient. Thus, "Possessor of Bounty" (ذو الطول) is one of His Beautiful Names meaning "the Bestower of Favors" (المنعم), but it is more specific than "the Bestower of Favors" because it does not include short-lived blessings.

The mention of these four names: "Forgiver of sin," "Accepter of repentance," "Severe in retribution," and "Possessor of bounty" after the name "the All-knowing" is to indicate that the revelation of this Book—which contains His true call based on knowledge—is established upon the foundation of what the meanings of these four names necessitate.

This is because the human world, while united as a single group in receiving divine bounty and enjoying His continuous and successive blessings throughout the life of this world, is divided regarding the life of the Hereafter into two parts and branches into two paths: the felicitous and the wretched. Allah, the Glorified, is All-knowing of the details of His creation—and how could He not know when He is their Creator and Fashioner? The necessity of His being the Forgiver of sin and Accepter of repentance is that He forgives those prepared for forgiveness and accepts the repentance of those who turn to Him. The necessity of His being severe in retribution is that He punishes those who deserve it.

The necessity of this is that He guides people to the path of felicity, as He said: إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا لَلْهُدَىٰ وَإِنَّ لَنَا لَلْآخِرَةَ وَالْأُولَىٰ "Indeed it is up to Us to guide, and to Us belong the Hereafter and the world" (92:13-14), and He said: وَعَلَى اللَّهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ "With Allah rests the [guidance to the] straight way" (16:9).

This is so that people may be divided into two groups, and the felicitous may be distinguished from the wretched, and the guided from the astray, so that He may show mercy to the former and punish the latter.

Thus, the revelation of the Book from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-knowing, is based on His knowledge—which encompasses His creation—that they are in need of a call by which some are guided and others go astray by rejecting it, so that He may forgive some and punish others. They are also in need of it so that the order of their worldly life may be organized, allowing them to enjoy His bounty and blessing in this world and then in the Abode of Permanence.

This, then, is the status of His Book, revealed by His knowledge which is untainted by ignorance, and established upon the truth (الحق) which is unmixed with falsehood. How far removed is this from the denial of those who know only the outward appearance of life, and their disputation through falsehood to refute the truth.

This emphasis on knowledge is supported by what He will mention later regarding the angels' prayer for the believers' forgiveness: رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تَابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ "Our Lord! You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge. So forgive those who repent and follow Your way" (40:7). So reflect upon this.

His saying: لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ "There is no god except Him, and to Him is the return." The word of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — was mentioned to indicate the obligation of worshipping Him alone, so that the religious call through the revelation of the Book does not become futile. The mention of the fact that the destination and return of all is to Him—which is the Resurrection (البعث)—is to indicate that this is the primary reason and motive for believing in the Book and following what it calls to. For the belief in the Day of Reckoning is what entails fear and hope—fear of punishment and hope for reward—which are the drivers toward the worship of Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: مَا يُجَادِلُ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَلَا يَغْرُرْكَ تَقَلُّبُهُمْ فِي الْبِلَادِ "None dispute the signs of Allah except the faithless. So do not be deluded by their turnover in the land" (40:4). After mentioning the revelation of the Book and pointing to the dazzling proof of its truthfulness—derived from His noble attributes enumerated in the two verses, which indicate that it was revealed by His knowledge untainted by ignorance and by the truth that cannot be refuted by falsehood—He addressed the state of those who met His true proofs with their false disputation. He alluded to the fact that these are the people of punishment; they shall not escape, nor are they overlooked. For just as He revealed the Book to forgive sin and accept repentance, He also revealed it to punish the people of punishment. Therefore, their disputation should not grieve the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, nor should he be deluded by what he observes of their condition.

Thus, His saying: "None dispute the signs of Allah" (مَا يُجَادِلُ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ)—He did not say: "None dispute regarding it," meaning the Quran—to indicate that the disputation is regarding the truth to which the signs point insofar as they are signs. Furthermore, the target of their disputation is the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who is a caller to the truth indicated by the signs; thus, their disputation is to repel the truth, not to defend it. Moreover, the disputation in the following verse is restricted to falsehood for the purpose of refuting the truth.

Therefore, what is meant by "disputing the signs of Allah" is the disputation aimed at refuting and repelling them, which is the blameworthy kind. it does not include disputation to establish the truth and defend it. How could it, when He, the Glorified, commands His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to do so if it is a disputation in the best manner? The Exalted said: وَجَادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ "and dispute with them in a manner that is best" (16:125).

His saying: "except the faithless" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا). The apparent context suggests they are those in whose hearts disbelief has become firmly rooted, such that its removal is not expected. He said: "None dispute" (مَا يُجَادِلُ) using the present tense. Likewise, the apparent meaning of His saying: "So do not be deluded by their turnover in the land" (فَلَا يَغْرُرْكَ تَقَلُّبُهُمْ فِي الْبِلَادِ) is that it refers to the disbelievers contemporary to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, even if they were not from the people of Mecca.

Their "turnover in the land" (تقلبهم في البلاد) refers to their transition from one state of life to another and from one blessing to another in safety, health, and well-being. Directing the prohibition of delusion toward their turnover in the land is a metaphor for prohibiting the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from being deceived by what he sees of them, such that he might think they have frustrated Him, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَالْأَحْزَابُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ "The people of Noah and the factions after them denied before them..." (40:5) etc. This is in the position of answering what might occur to the mind—that they were arrogant and disputed the signs of Allah yet suffered no harm and were successful in that.

The summary of the answer is: The past nations, such as the people of Noah and the factions (الأحزاب) after them like ‘Ad, Thamud, the people of Lot, and others, preceded these people in similar conduct of denial and disputation through falsehood. They plotted against their messenger to seize him, so the punishment befell them. Likewise, the decree of torment has been passed against the disbelievers. Thus, the thought that these people have outstripped Allah in what He intends is a false delusion.

So His saying: "The people of Noah and the factions after them denied before them" is a rebuttal of the aforementioned potential objection, which is why it is presented as a separate sentence (الفصل). His saying: وَهَمَّتْ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ بِرَسُولِهِمْ لِيَأْخُذُوهُ "and every nation plotted against its apostle to seize him" (40:5). It is said: "He plotted against him" (هم به) meaning he intended him, and it is mostly used for an evil intention; i.e., they intended their messenger to seize him by killing, expulsion, or otherwise, as Allah has narrated in their stories.

His saying: وَجَادَلُوا بِالْبَاطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا بِهِ الْحَقَّ "and they disputed by means of falsehood to refute the truth thereby" (40:5). Refutation (الإدحاض) means removal and nullification. His saying: فَأَخَذْتُهُمْ "so I seized them" (40:5) means I punished them. There is a shift (التفات) here from the third person to the first person singular. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to indicate that their matter in this rebellion and arrogance is for Allah alone; no one intervenes between Him and them with help or intercession, as He said: فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصَادِ "So your Lord poured on them the whip of punishment. Indeed your Lord is in ambush" (89:13-14).

His saying: فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عِقَابِ "so how was My retribution?" (40:5) is to direct the mind of the addressee to what he knows of the manner of their destruction and the cutting off of their roots, so that he may recall the severity of what befell them, as Allah has narrated in their stories.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذَٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ "Thus was the word of your Lord fulfilled against the faithless: that they are the inmates of the Fire" (40:6). The apparent context is that the point of comparison is what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding their seizure and punishment. "Those who disbelieve" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) refers to disbelievers in general from the past. The meaning is: Just as Allah seized the deniers of the past with the punishment of this world, likewise His word has been fulfilled against disbelievers in general with the punishment of the Hereafter; and the disbelievers among your people are among them.

It has been said that "those who disbelieve" refers to the disbelievers of Mecca, but the context does not support this, and the comparison would not be free from inconsistency.

In His saying: "the word of your Lord" (كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ) rather than "My word," there is a solace for the soul of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and support for him by indicating that the Pillar upon which he leans is the Severe and Mighty One.

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ ٱلْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ رَّحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا فَٱغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٧)
Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful: ‘Our Lord! You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge. So forgive those who repent and follow Your way and save them from the punishment of hell. (7)
رَبَّنَا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ٱلَّتِى وَعَدتَّهُمْ وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَأَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٨)
Our Lord! Admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them, along with whoever is righteous among their forebears, their spouses and their descendants. Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise. (8)
وَقِهِمُ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ وَمَن تَقِ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمْتَهُۥ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٩)
Save them from the ills [of the Day of Retribution]; and whomever You save from the ills that day, You will have had mercy upon him, and that is the great triumph.’ (9)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُنَادَوْنَ لَمَقْتُ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ مِن مَّقْتِكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ (١٠)
It will be proclaimed to the faithless: ‘Surely, Allah’s outrage [towards you] is greater than your outrage towards yourselves, as you were invited to faith, but you disbelieved.’ (10)
قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَمَتَّنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَٱعْتَرَفْنَا بِذُنُوبِنَا فَهَلْ إِلَىٰ خُرُوجٍ مِّن سَبِيلٍ (١١)
They will say, ‘Our Lord! Twice did You make us die, and twice did You give us life. We admit our sins. Is there any way out [from this plight]?’ (11)
ذَٰلِكُم بِأَنَّهُۥٓ إِذَا دُعِىَ ٱللَّهُ وَحْدَهُۥ كَفَرْتُمْ ۖ وَإِن يُشْرَكْ بِهِۦ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ ۚ فَٱلْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْعَلِىِّ ٱلْكَبِيرِ (١٢)
[They will be told,] ‘This [plight of yours] is because, when Allah was invoked alone, you would disbelieve, but if partners were ascribed to Him you would believe. So the judgment belongs to Allah, the All-exalted, the All-great.’ (12)

(Commentary)

When the Glorified mentioned the denial of those who disbelieve and their arguing against the signs of Allah with falsehood, He alluded to the fact that they cannot frustrate Him, nor are they overlooked. Rather, they are intended by this invitation, and the concern regarding them is that they be distinguished so that the word of punishment may be justified against them and they may be penalized. He then returned to the beginning of the discourse, in which He indicated that the revelation of the Book and the establishment of the invitation were for the forgiveness of a group and the acceptance of their repentance, and for the punishment of others. Thus, He mentioned that people, in the face of this invitation, are of two groups: a group for whom the bearers of the Throne and those around it from among the angels seek forgiveness—these are the repentant ones to Allah who follow His path, along with the righteous among their fathers, spouses, and progeny—and a group who are detested and punished, and they are those who disbelieve in monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ — "Those who bear the Throne and those who are around it celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him" (40:7), until the end of the verse. The Glorified did not identify who these bearers of the Throne are, nor is there an explicit statement in His speech that they are from the angels. However, the conjunction of His saying: وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ (and those who are around it) with them—while He has said regarding those around it: وَتَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ — "And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne" (39:75)—suggests that the bearers of the Throne are also from the angels.

The detailed discussion regarding the meaning of the Throne (العرش) has already been presented in the eighth volume of this book.

Thus, His saying: الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ refers to the angels who bear the Throne from which the commands appear and the divine decrees are issued, by which the world is governed; and those around the Throne are the angels who are the near-stationed (المقربون) among them.

His saying: يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ means they declare the transcendence of Allah, the Glorified, while their glorification of Him is accompanied by praise for their Lord. They exalt Him, the Exalted, above everything that does not befit the sanctuary of His holiness—including the existence of a partner in His kingdom—and they praise Him for His actions and His governance.

His saying: وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ means their faith in Him—given this state where the Throne of sovereignty and governance belongs to Allah and they are its bearers or those circling around it to receive commands, exalting Him above every deficiency and praising Him for His acts—signifies faith in His oneness in His Lordship (الربوبية) and His Divinity (الألوهية). In mentioning the Throne and attributing glorification, praise, and faith to the angels, there is a refutation of the polytheists, as they consider the near-stationed angels to be partners with Allah in His Lordship and Divinity, taking them as lords and gods whom they worship.

His saying: وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا means they ask Allah, the Glorified, to forgive those who believe.

His saying: رَبَّنا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَحْمَةً وَعِلْماً — "Our Lord, You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge" (40:7), etc., is a narration of the text of their seeking forgiveness. They began it by praising Him, the Exalted, for the vastness of His mercy and knowledge. They mentioned mercy and coupled it with knowledge because, through His mercy, He bestows favors upon every needy one—mercy being the principle of the emanation of every blessing—and through His knowledge, He knows the need of every needy one who is prepared for mercy.

His saying: فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ — "So forgive those who repent and follow Your way, and save them from the punishment of hell" (40:7), is a derivation from the praise they offered regarding the vastness of His mercy and knowledge. What is meant by the "way" (السبيل) they followed is what was legislated for them of the religion (الدين), which is Islam (الإسلام), and their following it is the application of their deeds to it. Thus, what is meant by their repentance (توبتهم) is their return to Him, the Exalted, through faith. The meaning is: forgive those who returned to You by believing in Your oneness and traversing Your path, which is Islam, and protect them from the punishment of hell, which is the ultimate goal and purpose of forgiveness.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدْتَهُمْ — "Our Lord, admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them" (40:8), until the end of the verse. The repetition of the call with the word "Our Lord" (ربنا) is for increased entreaty. What is meant by the "promise" is His promise to them through the tongues of His messengers and in His books.

His saying: وَمَنْ صَلَحَ مِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَأَزْواجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ — "along with whoever is righteous among their forefathers, their spouses and their descendants" (40:8), is conjoined to the position of the pronoun in His saying: وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ (and admit them). What is meant by "righteousness" (الصلوح) is the suitability for entering Paradise. The meaning is: and admit those who are suitable for entering Paradise from among their fathers, spouses, and progeny into the Gardens of Eden.

Furthermore, it is known from the context of the verses that their seeking of forgiveness is for the general body of believers. It is also known that they divided them into two groups: they divided them into those who repented and followed the way of Allah, whom Allah promised the Gardens of Eden, and those who were righteous. They made the first group the followed and the second group the followers.

It appears from this that the first group are those who are complete in faith and action, as necessitated by the reality of the meaning of their saying: لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ (for those who repent and follow Your way). Thus, they mentioned them and asked Him to forgive them and fulfill for them what He promised them of the Gardens of Eden. The second group is below these in rank, consisting of those who did not complete their faith and action—those with deficient faith, the oppressed (المستضعف), and those with bad deeds among the relatives of the first group. So they mentioned them and asked Him, the Exalted, to join them with the first group of complete believers in their gardens and to protect them from evil deeds.

Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "As for those who have faith and whose descendants follow them in faith, We will unite their descendants with them, and We will not stint them anything of their deeds" (52:21). However, the verse we are currently discussing is broader and more inclusive, as it includes fathers and spouses, unlike the verse in Surah al-Tur. Moreover, what is stipulated here is "righteousness" (الصلوح), which is more general than the "faith" (الإيمان) stipulated in the verse of al-Tur.

His saying: إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — "Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise" (40:8), is a justification for their saying: فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا (so forgive those who repent), until the end of their request. What the apparent context would seem to require is to say: "Indeed You are the All-Forgiving, the All-Merciful." However, it shifted to mentioning the two attributes: the All-Mighty (العزيز) and the All-Wise (الحكيم), because at the opening of their request, there occurred the praise of Him, the Exalted, with their saying: رَبَّنا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَحْمَةً وَعِلْماً. The necessity of the vastness of mercy—which is the generality of bestowing—is that He has the right to give what He wills to whom He wills and withhold what He wills from whom He wills; and this is the meaning of might (العزة), which is the power to bestow and withhold. The necessity of the vastness of knowledge of everything is that knowledge permeates all aspects of the action, such that ignorance does not enter any part of it; and its necessity is the perfection of the action, which is wisdom (الحكمة).

Thus, His saying: إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ is in the sense of seeking intercession (الاستشفاع) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — through the vastness of His mercy and the vastness of His knowledge, both mentioned at the opening of the request as a preparation and preamble for mentioning the need, which is forgiveness and Paradise.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقِهِمُ السَّيِّئاتِ وَمَنْ تَقِ السَّيِّئاتِ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمْتَهُ — "And save them from the ills; and whomever You save from the ills on that day, You will have had mercy upon him" (40:9), etc. The apparent context is that the pronoun in قِهِمُ (save them) refers to both those who repented and those who were righteous.

What is meant by "ills" (السيئات)—according to what has been said—are the consequences of sins, which are their punishment. The consequences are called "ills" because the recompense for an ill is an ill. The Exalted said: وَجَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها — "The recompense of an ill is an ill like it" (42:40).

It has also been said: what is meant by "ills" are the acts of disobedience and sins themselves, and the speech assumes a suppressed word, the estimation being: "save them from the recompense of ills" or "the punishment of ills."

The apparent meaning is that the verse is among those indicating that the recompense is the deeds themselves, their good and their evil. Similar statements have been repeated in His speech, such as His saying: إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "You are only being requited for what you used to do" (66:7).

In any case, the "ills" from which they asked for protection are the horrors and hardships they will face on the Day of Resurrection, other than the punishment of hell. Thus, there is no repetition in their two sayings: وَقِهْمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ (and save them from the punishment of hell) and وَقِهِمُ السَّيِّئاتِ (and save them from the ills).

It has also been said: what is meant by "ills" are the sins themselves in this world, and their saying: يَوْمَئِذٍ (on that day) refers to the world. The meaning would be: "and protect them from committing sins and engaging in them in the world through Your success-granting (توفيق)."

However, the context supports that what is meant by "on that day" is the Day of Resurrection, as evidenced by their saying: وَقِهِمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ and their saying: وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ, etc. Thus, the truth is that what is meant by "ills" are the horrors and hardships that will appear to people on the Day of Resurrection.

From these verses containing the prayer and request of the angels, it appears:

First: That part of the etiquette of prayer (الدعاء) is to begin with His praise and glorification, then to mention the need, and then to seek intercession through His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) that are appropriate to it.

Second: That the request for forgiveness precedes the request for Paradise. The mention of forgiveness before Paradise has occurred frequently in His speech when they are mentioned together. This is in accordance with rational consideration, for the attainment of readiness for any blessing occurs by the removal of the obstacle before the attainment of the blessing itself.

Some have mentioned that in His saying: فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا, the verse indicates that the dropping of punishment upon repentance is a favor (تفضل) from Allah, the Exalted. For if it were obligatory, there would be no need for their request; rather, Allah, the Glorified, would inevitably do it.

However, the fact that the issuance of an act from Him, the Exalted, is necessary does not negate the validity of requesting and seeking it from Him, as evidenced by their saying after seeking forgiveness: رَبَّنا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدْتَهُمْ. They asked for Paradise for them while acknowledging that Allah had promised it to them, and His promise is obligatory to fulfill, for He does not break His promise. More explicit than this verse is His saying narrating from the believers: رَبَّنا وَآتِنا ما وَعَدْتَنا عَلى رُسُلِكَ وَلا تُخْزِنا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّكَ لا تُخْلِفُ الْمِيعادَ — "Our Lord, give us what You have promised us through Your apostles, and do not disgrace us on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed You do not break Your promise" (3:194).

The acceptance of repentance is among the things Allah has made obligatory upon Himself and has made a right for the repentant ones. The Exalted said: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ فَأُولئِكَ يَتُوبُ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ — "Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance and then repent promptly. It is such whose repentance Allah will accept" (4:17). Thus, seeking every right that Allah has made obligatory upon Himself—such as asking for forgiveness for the repentant—is in reality a return to Him to seek the fulfillment of what He promised and an expression of longing to attain His honor.

Likewise, His favor (تفضل) does not necessitate that the act be merely permissible to issue and not obligatory. Every gift of His gifts is a favor, whether its issuance was obligatory or not. For if an act among His acts were obligatory to issue from Him, its obligation upon Him would not be by the influence of another upon Him or by coercion, as He is the influencer in everything and nothing else influences Him. Rather, that would be by an obligation from Him, the Exalted, upon Himself. Its meaning boils down to His decree (قضاء) to perform an act and to emanate a gift as a decisive decree. Thus, the Glorified only performs it by a volition from Himself, transcendent above being compelled by another, doing so as a favor. So the act is a favor from Him even if its issuance is obligatory. As for if the act were not obligatory to issue, its being a favor is even clearer.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يُنادَوْنَ لَمَقْتُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ مَقْتِكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى الْإِيمانِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ — "Indeed those who disbelieve will be told, ‘Allah’s abhorrence [of you] is greater than your abhorrence of yourselves, for you would disbelieve when you were summoned to faith’" (40:10). Abhorrence (المقت) is the most intense hatred. When He mentioned the believers with some of what they have by virtue of their faith, He returned to mentioning the disbelievers with some of what is against them by virtue of their disbelief.

The apparent meaning of the verse and the following verse is that this mentioned call is addressed to them in the Hereafter after entering the Fire, when they taste the punishment for their disbelief. It then becomes clear to them that their disbelief in the world—when they were being called by the prophets to faith—was an abhorrence and intense hatred from them toward themselves, as they brought themselves thereby to the place of permanent destruction.

They are called from the side of Allah, the Glorified, and it is said to them: "I swear, Allah's abhorrence and intense hatred for you is greater than your abhorrence of yourselves and your intense hatred for them when you were being called"—narrating a past state—"to faith by the prophets, yet you disbelieved."

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا رَبَّنا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ فَاعْتَرَفْنا بِذُنُوبِنا فَهَلْ إِلى خُرُوجٍ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ — "They will say, ‘Our Lord! Twice did You make us die, and twice did You give us life. We admit our sins. Is there any way out?’" (40:11). The context of the verse and what precedes it suggests that they say this after hearing the previous call. They only say it while they are in the Fire, as evidenced by their saying: فَهَلْ إِلى خُرُوجٍ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ (Is there any way out?).

Presenting this confession from them is a kind of seeking a cause and a means to escape the punishment, but there is no time for escape. This is because, while they were in the world, they were in doubt regarding the Resurrection and the return to Allah, so they denied it and forgot the Day of Reckoning. Forgetting that was the cause of their indulgence in sins and their headlong rush into acts of disobedience. Forgetting the Day of Reckoning is the key to every disobedience and misguidance. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ — "Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (38:26).

Then, when Allah caused them to die death after death and gave them life life after life, their doubt regarding the matter of the Resurrection and the return to Allah vanished due to what they witnessed of survival after death and life after life. They used to think that death was annihilation, saying: "It is nothing but our life in this world, and we shall not be resurrected."

In short, doubt vanished from them with the attainment of certainty (اليقين), but the sins and acts of disobedience remained. Therefore, they sought a means to escape the punishment through confession. Sometimes they confessed to the attainment of certainty, as Allah narrated from them in His saying: وَلَوْ تَرى إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ — "Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [saying], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard. Send us back so that we may act righteously. Indeed we are now certain’" (32:12). At other times, they confessed to their sins, as in the verse under discussion. They used to think they were free and independent in their will and actions, entitled to wish what they wished and do what they did, with no reckoning and no sin.

From this, the reason for the sequence of their saying: فَاعْتَرَفْنا بِذُنُوبِنا (so we admit our sins) following their saying: أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ (Twice did You make us die, and twice did You give us life) becomes clear. The confession is, in reality, sequenced upon the attainment of certainty regarding the Return (المعاد), which necessitates the knowledge that their deviations from the way of Allah were misguidance and sins.

What is meant by their saying: أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ—as has been said—is the causing of death from the life of this world and the giving of life for the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection, then the causing of death from the intermediate realm and the giving of life for the Reckoning on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, the verse refers to the death after the life of this world and the death after the life of the intermediate realm, and to the life in the intermediate realm and the life for the Day of Resurrection. Were it not for the life of the intermediate realm, the second death would not be realized, because both death and life depend for their realization on the precedence of their opposite.

They did not mention the life of this world, nor did they say: "and You gave us life thrice," even though it was a giving of life because it occurred after the death which is the state of the non-existence of the spirit's entry. This is because their intention was to mention the giving of life which is the cause of certainty in the Return, which is the life in the intermediate realm and then in the Resurrection. As for the worldly life, although it was a giving of life, it did not by itself necessitate certainty in the Return, for they were in doubt regarding the Return while they were alive in the world.

From the preceding explanation, the invalidity of the objection becomes clear—the objection that if the two lives meant those in the intermediate realm and the Hereafter, it would have been necessary to say: "You made us die twice and gave us life thrice," since the intention is only to mention the deaths and lives they passed through, which are two deaths and three lives.

The answer is that the intention is not merely to mention the deaths and lives they passed through in whatever manner they were, but rather to mention those among them that bequeathed certainty in the Return, and the worldly life is not of this description.

It has also been said: what is meant by the first death is the state of the sperm before the entry of the spirit, and by the first life what is the state of the human after its entry; by the second death his death in the world, and by the second life his being given life by the Resurrection for the Reckoning on the Day of Resurrection. The verse would then correspond to what is in His saying, the Exalted: كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِاللهِ وَكُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ — "How can you deny Allah? You were lifeless and He gave you life, then He will make you die, then He will bring you to life" (2:28).

However, when they sensed that "death" (الإماتة) does not truly apply to the state of the human before the entry of the spirit into his body—because it depends on the precedence of life—they resorted to strange interpretations to justify it. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to al-Kashshaf and its commentaries.

Furthermore, you have already known that their mention of the deaths and lives they passed through is an allusion to the causes of their attaining certainty in the Return, and the worldly life and the death preceding it have no effect on that.

It has also been said: the first life is in the world and the second in the grave; the first death is in the world and the second in the grave. There is no mention in the verse of the life of the Day of Resurrection. This is refuted by what was mentioned before: that the worldly life has no connection to the purpose, so there is no reason to mention it, whereas the life on the Day of Resurrection is the opposite of that.

It has also been said: what is meant by the two lives is the life of the Resurrection and the life preceding it. The life of the Resurrection is of two types: life in the grave and life at the Resurrection. This division was not addressed in the verse, so the verse would include all three lives and both deaths.

This is refuted by what refutes the two previous views, in addition to the point that the mention of the second death which is in the grave is evidence that the division is intended, and what is meant is numerical plurality (التعدد الشخصي), not generic (النوعي).

It has also been said: what is meant is the giving of life to the souls in the world of particles (عالم الذر), then death, then life in the world, then death, then life for the Resurrection. This is refuted by what refutes its predecessors.

It has also been said: what is meant by the dual form is repetition, as in His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ — "Then look again" (67:4). The meaning would be: "You made us die death after death and gave us life life after life."

This was objected to on the grounds that it would only be valid if the statement were: "You made us die two deaths and gave us life two lives" or "two times," for example. But what was said is the number itself, and it does not admit that possibility, as has been said regarding His saying: إِلهَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ — "two gods" (16:51).

Their saying: فَهَلْ إِلى خُرُوجٍ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ is a prayer and a request in the form of an interrogation. In the indefiniteness of "exit" (خروج) and "way" (سبيل), there is an indication of their satisfaction with any kind of exit through any possible way. For exhaustion has reached its peak with them, and the day is a day when all means have been cut off from them, so there is no means whose effect can be hoped for in their escape from the punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكُمْ بِأَنَّهُ إِذا دُعِيَ اللهُ وَحْدَهُ كَفَرْتُمْ وَإِنْ يُشْرَكْ بِهِ تُؤْمِنُوا — "This is because when Allah was invoked alone, you would disbelieve, but if partners were ascribed to Him, you would believe" (40:12), etc. This is a discourse of severity toward the disbelievers, its place being the Day of Resurrection. It is possible that its place is the world, addressed to them for the purpose of deterring them from polytheism (الشرك).

The indication in His saying: ذلِكُمْ (This) is to the hardship they are in. In His saying: وَإِنْ يُشْرَكْ بِهِ (but if partners were ascribed to Him), there is an indication of persistence. The speech is intended to explain their stubbornness against the truth and their enmity toward His oneness. They disbelieve in everything in which a trace of monotheism appears, and they believe in everything that bears the mark of polytheism. They do not observe any right for Allah, nor do they respect any side of His. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, forbids them His mercy and does not observe any side for them in His judgment.

In this sense, His saying: فَالْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ الْعَلِيِّ الْكَبِيرِ — "So the judgment belongs to Allah, the All-exalted, the All-great" (40:12), connects to the beginning of the verse and is derived from it. It is as if it were said: "Since you have cut yourselves off from Allah entirely, and disbelieved in everything He desires, and believed in everything He dislikes, He cuts Himself off from you and judges concerning you as He judges, without any consideration for your state."

Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying: نَسُوا اللهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ — "They forgot Allah, so He forgot them" (9:67). The sentence—I mean His saying: فَالْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ الْعَلِيِّ الْكَبِيرِ—is specific according to the context, even if it is general in itself. In it is a threat, and the threat is emphasized by its conclusion with the two names: the All-Exalted (العلي) and the All-Great (الكبير).

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَيُنَزِّلُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ رِزْقًا ۚ وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَن يُنِيبُ (١٣)
It is He who shows you His signs and sends down provision for you from the sky. Yet no one takes admonition except those who return penitently [to Allah]. (13)
فَٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (١٤)
So supplicate Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him, though the faithless should be averse. (14)
رَفِيعُ ٱلدَّرَجَـٰتِ ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ يُلْقِى ٱلرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ لِيُنذِرَ يَوْمَ ٱلتَّلَاقِ (١٥)
Raiser of ranks, Lord of the Throne, He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever of His servants that He wishes, that he may warn [people] of the Day of Encounter. (15)
يَوْمَ هُم بَـٰرِزُونَ ۖ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَىْءٌ ۚ لِّمَنِ ٱلْمُلْكُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ لِلَّهِ ٱلْوَٰحِدِ ٱلْقَهَّارِ (١٦)
The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah. ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’ (16)
ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ ۚ لَا ظُلْمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (١٧)
‘Today every soul shall be requited for what it has earned. There will be no injustice today. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning.’ (17)
وَأَنذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔازِفَةِ إِذِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ لَدَى ٱلْحَنَاجِرِ كَـٰظِمِينَ ۚ مَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ (١٨)
Warn them of the Approaching Day, when the hearts will be at the throats, choking with suppressed agony, [and] the wrongdoers will have no sympathizer, nor any intercessor who might be heard. (18)
يَعْلَمُ خَآئِنَةَ ٱلْأَعْيُنِ وَمَا تُخْفِى ٱلصُّدُورُ (١٩)
He knows the treachery of the eyes, and what the breasts hide. (19)
وَٱللَّهُ يَقْضِى بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَقْضُونَ بِشَىْءٍ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (٢٠)
Allah judges with justice, while those whom they invoke besides Him do not judge by anything. Indeed it is Allah who is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (20)

(Commentary)

This is an argumentation for monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and a warning following the division of people into those who return to Allah and follow His path, and those who deny the signs and argue with falsehood.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِهِ "It is He who shows you His signs" until the end of the verse. What is meant by the signs (الآيات) are the marks (العلائم) and proofs (الحجج) indicating His oneness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية), as evidenced by the subsequent derivation of His saying: فَادْعُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ "So supplicate Allah, making religion pure for Him" from it. The signs are general, encompassing the cosmic signs (الآيات الكونية) witnessed in the world by every person of sound perception, as well as the signs that occur at the hands of the messengers and the established proofs through the way of revelation (الوحي).

The sentence contains an argument: for if there were a deity whose worship was obligatory upon man, and whose worship constituted perfection and felicity for man, it would be necessary, within the perfection of governance (التدبير) and complete providence (العناية), that He guide man toward Himself. The One whose Lordship and Divinity are indicated by the cosmic signs, and whose indication is supported by the messengers and prophets through their call and the bringing of signs, is Allah, the Glorified. As for their deities whom they call upon besides Allah, there is no sign from them indicating anything. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the sole Deity, having no partner. To this argument, Ali (peace be upon him) refers in what is narrated from him: "If your Lord had a partner, his messengers would have come to you."

And His saying: وَيُنَزِّلُ لَكُمْ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ رِزْقًا "and sends down for you provision from the sky" is another argument for His oneness, the Exalted, from the perspective of provision (الرزق). For the provisioning of the servants is among the affairs of Lordship and Divinity, and provision is from Allah rather than their partners; thus, He is the Lord and Deity, not them.

The exegetes have interpreted "provision" as rain and "the sky" as the direction of height. It is not improbable that "provision" refers to the very things by which one is sustained, and their "sending down from the sky" refers to their emergence from the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — into the manifest world (الشهادة), according to what is conveyed by His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ There is not a thing but that its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure. (15:21).

And His saying: وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَنْ يُنِيبُ "and no one takes admonition except him who returns penitently" is a parenthetical statement explaining that the attainment of remembrance (التذكر) through these proofs is only the characteristic of one of the two aforementioned groups, namely the penitent (المنيبون) who return to their Lord, excluding the faithless (الكافرين) who argue. For disbelief (الكفر) and obstinate denial (الجحود) nullify the capacity for remembrance through proof and the following of truth.

His saying, the Exalted: فَادْعُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْكَافِرُونَ So supplicate Allah, making religion pure for Him, though the faithless should be averse. (40:14). It is more appropriate to the context that the address be general to the believers and others, derived from the preceding argument, except that it does not include the disbelievers mentioned at the end of the verse, who are the deniers that argue with falsehood.

It is as if it were said: Since the signs indicate His oneness, the Exalted, and He is the Provider, then those other than the disbelievers who denied and argued must supplicate Allah, making religion pure for Him. As for the disbelievers who loathe monotheism, there is no hope for them; no sign benefits them and no proof convinces them. So worship Him with sincerity (الإخلاص) and let the disbelievers loathe that.

His saying, the Exalted: رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجَاتِ ذُو الْعَرْشِ يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَى مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ "Exalter of ranks, Lord of the Throne, He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants" etc. These are three attributes of Him, the Exalted, each being a predicate after a predicate for the pronoun in His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِهِ "It is He who shows you His signs." The verse and what follows it are directed toward warning (الإنذار).

Various interpretations have been offered for His saying: رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجَاتِ "Exalter of ranks." It has been said to mean: the One who raises the ranks of the prophets and saints in Paradise. It has also been said: the One who raised the seven heavens from which the angels ascend to His Throne. It has been said: the One whose Throne's points of ascent are high. And it has been said: it is a metaphor for the loftiness of His status and authority (السلطان).

What reflection (التدبر) yields is that the verse and what follows it describe His kingdom, the Exalted, over His creation: that He has a Throne (العرش) in which the reins of the affairs of creation are gathered, and from which the command (الأمر) descends, being exalted by high ranks (درجات رفيعة) which are the levels of His creation. Perhaps these are the heavens which He described in His speech as the dwellings of His angels and that His command descends between them, and they are what veil His Throne from the people.

Furthermore, He has a day, which is the Day of Encounter (يوم التلاق), in which the veil between Him and the people is lifted by the removal of the covering from their insights, the folding of the heavens with His right hand, and the manifestation of His Throne to them. It will then be disclosed to them that He is the King over all things; there is no kingdom except His kingdom, and He shall judge between them.

Thus, what is meant by "ranks" (الدرجات) are the levels from which one ascends to His Throne. His saying: رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجَاتِ ذُو الْعَرْشِ "Exalter of ranks, Lord of the Throne" becomes a metaphorical allusion to the transcendence of the Throne of His kingdom above the level of creation, and its absence and concealment from them before the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) by high ranks and distant stages.

And His saying: يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَى مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ "He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants" is an allusion to the matter of the message (الرسالة) whose function is warning. The qualification of the Spirit (الروح) by His saying: مِنْ أَمْرِهِ "of His command" is proof that what is meant by it is the Spirit He mentioned in His saying: قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’ (17:85), which is that which accompanies the angels of revelation, as indicated by His saying: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَى مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ أَنْ أَنْذِرُوا He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants: ‘Warn...’ (16:2).

Thus, what is meant by "casting the Spirit" upon whomever He wishes is sending it down with the angels of revelation upon him. What is meant by His saying: مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ "whomever He wishes of His servants" are the messengers whom Allah has chosen for His message. Regarding the meaning of the Spirit cast upon the Prophet, there are other opinions that are not worth considering.

And His saying: لِيُنْذِرَ يَوْمَ التَّلَاقِ "that he may warn of the Day of Encounter." This is the Day of Resurrection; it is so named because of the meeting of the creatures therein, or the meeting of the Creator and the created, or the meeting of the inhabitants of heaven and earth, or the meeting of the oppressor and the oppressed, or the meeting of a man and his deeds. Each of these views has its proponents.

The second view may be supported by the repeated mention in His speech, the Exalted, of the "encounter" (اللقاء), such as His saying: بِلِقَاءِ رَبِّهِمْ لَكَافِرُونَ are indeed disbelievers in the encounter with their Lord (30:8), and His saying: إِنَّهُمْ مُلَاقُو رَبِّهِمْ they will indeed encounter their Lord (11:29), and His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسَانُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَى رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَاقِيهِ O man! You are laboring toward your Lord laboriously, and you will encounter Him. (84:6). The meaning of the encounter (اللقاء) is the severing of distracting causes (الأسباب), the manifestation that Allah is the Manifest Truth, and their emergence before Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ هُمْ بَارِزُونَ لَا يَخْفَى عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ "The day when they will emerge: nothing about them will be hidden from Allah" etc., is an explanation of the Day of Encounter. The meaning of their "emergence" (بروزهم) before Allah is the manifestation of that to them and the removal of the illusory causes that used to pull them toward themselves, veiling them from their Lord and making them heedless of the encompassment of His kingdom, His exclusivity in judgment, and His uniqueness in Lordship and Divinity.

Thus, His saying: يَوْمَ هُمْ بَارِزُونَ "The day when they will emerge" is an allusion to the removal of every veiling cause. His saying: لَا يَخْفَى عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ "nothing about them will be hidden from Allah" is an explanation of the meaning of their emergence before Allah and a clarification; for their hearts and deeds are before the eye of Allah, and their outward and inward, what they remembered and what they forgot, are exposed and not concealed.

And His saying: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’ (40:16). This is a question and an answer from His side, the Glorified, through which the reality of the Day is explained: it is the manifestation of His kingdom and authority, the Exalted, over the creation absolutely.

In His being described as "the One, the All-paramount" (الواحد القهار), there is a justification for the restriction of sovereignty to Him. For since He has vanquished (قهر) everything, He owns it and dominates it by stripping it of independence, and since He is One, the sovereignty belongs to Him alone.

His saying, the Exalted: الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ لَا ظُلْمَ الْيَوْمَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسَابِ Today every soul shall be recompensed for what it has earned. There will be no injustice today. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning. (40:17). The preposition "with" (بـ) in "for what it has earned" (بما كسبت) denotes connection, and the intent is to explain the characteristic of the Day: that every soul is recompensed with the very thing it earned, so its recompense is its deed. The Exalted said: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ O you who have disbelieved! Do not make any excuses today. You are only being recompensed for what you used to do. (66:7).

And His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسَابِ "Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning" is a justification for the negation of injustice in His saying: لَا ظُلْمَ الْيَوْمَ "There will be no injustice today." That is, He, the Exalted, is swift in reckoning; the reckoning of one soul does not distract Him from the reckoning of another such that He might err and recompense a soul with other than its due, thereby treating it unjustly.

This justification looks toward the negation of injustice arising from error. As for injustice arising from intent and knowledge, its absence is a foregone conclusion, because since the recompense is the deed itself, injustice is inconceivable.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْآزِفَةِ إِذِ الْقُلُوبُ لَدَى الْحَنَاجِرِ كَاظِمِينَ "And warn them of the Approaching Day, when the hearts will be at the throats, choking with grief" until the end of the verse. "The Approaching" (الآزفة) is one of the descriptions of the Resurrection, meaning that which is near and imminent. The Exalted said: إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُ بَعِيدًا وَنَرَاهُ قَرِيبًا Indeed they see it to be far off, and We see it to be near. (70:6-7).

And His saying: إِذِ الْقُلُوبُ لَدَى الْحَنَاجِرِ كَاظِمِينَ "when the hearts will be at the throats, choking with grief." The "throats" (الحناجر) is the plural of throat (حنجرة), which is the top of the windpipe from the outside. The hearts being at the throats is a metaphor for extreme fear, as if they shift from their place and reach the throats due to the intensity of terror. "Choking with grief" (كاظمين) is from "choking" (الكظم), which is the intensity of sorrow.

And His saying: مَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ The wrongdoers will have no sympathetic friend, nor any intercessor who might be heard. (40:18). The "sympathetic friend" (الحميم) is the close relative; that is, they have no relative who would stand to help them out of the zeal of kinship. The Exalted said: فَلَا أَنْسَابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ there will be no kinship between them on that day (23:101). Nor is there an intercessor (شفيع) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — whose intercession is obeyed.

His saying, the Exalted: يَعْلَمُ خَائِنَةَ الْأَعْيُنِ وَمَا تُخْفِي الصُّدُورُ He knows the treachery of the eyes, and what the breasts conceal. (40:19). It has been said: "treachery" (خائنة) is a verbal noun like "treason" (الخيانة), similar to "lie" (كاذبة) and "vain talk" (لاغية) meaning lying and vanity. What is meant by the treachery of the eyes is not every sin of theirs, but rather the sins that do not appear to others, such as a stolen glance, as evidenced by its mention alongside what the breasts conceal.

It has also been said: "the treachery of the eyes" (خائنة الأعين) is a case of an attribute being added to the described object, which necessitates that "knowledge" (العلم) here means "cognition" (المعرفة), and the meaning would be: He knows the treacherous eyes. The first view is the correct one.

And His saying: وَمَا تُخْفِي الصُّدُورُ "and what the breasts conceal" refers to what the soul keeps secret and hides of the forms of disbelief, hypocrisy, and the dispositions of sins.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ يَقْضِي بِالْحَقِّ وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ لَا يَقْضُونَ بِشَيْءٍ Allah judges with justice, while those they invoke besides Him do not judge by anything. (40:20). This is another argument for His uniqueness, the Exalted, in Divinity, which He established after mentioning the exclusivity of sovereignty to Him on the Day of Resurrection and His knowledge of the treachery of the eyes and what the breasts conceal, as a preparation and introduction.

Its summary is that it is a necessary requirement in Divinity that the Deity judge among His servants and between them. Allah, the Glorified, is the One who judges between the creation and among them on the Day of Resurrection, while those they invoke besides Him do not judge by anything because they are owned servants who possess nothing.

Among His judgment, the Exalted, is His governance of the particulars of His servants' affairs through creation after creation, for that is the manifestation of judgment (القضاء) and decree (الحكم). The Exalted said: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. (36:82), and He said: إِذَا قَضَى أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (3:47). Since no one besides Him, the Exalted, has a share in creation, no one has a share in judgment.

Also among His judgment, the Exalted, is the legislation of religion and His approval of it as a path for Himself. The Exalted said: وَقَضَى رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ Your Lord has decided that you shall not worship anyone except Him (17:23).

And His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ Indeed Allah is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (40:20). That is, He possesses the reality of knowledge of things heard and seen by His essence (لذاته), while no one else possesses any of that except what Allah has granted and permitted, not by their own essence.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَى مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ "He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants," he said: "The Holy Spirit (روح القدس) — and it is specific to the Messenger of Allah and the Imams, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله."

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of transmission from Hafs ibn Ghiyath, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Day of Encounter (يوم التلاق) is the day when the inhabitants of heaven and the inhabitants of earth meet."

I say: It was also narrated by al-Qummi in his Tafsir as an unattributed and broken narration.

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Faddal, from al-Rida, from his fathers, from Ali (peace be upon him), in a narration where he said: "And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ 'To whom does the sovereignty belong today?' Then the spirits of His prophets, His messengers, and His proofs speak, saying: لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ 'To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!' Then Allah, majestic is His majesty, says: الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ 'Today every soul shall be recompensed for what it has earned' the verse."

In Nahj al-Balagha: "And He, the Glorified, shall return after the annihilation of the world alone, with nothing alongside Him, just as He was before its beginning; so shall He be after its annihilation, without time (وقت), period (زمان), moment (حين), or place (مكان). At that point, terms and times shall cease to exist, and years and hours shall vanish. There shall be nothing but Allah, the One, the All-paramount, to whom is the return of all affairs. Without power from them was the beginning of their creation, and without resistance from them was their annihilation. Had they the power to resist, their existence would have endured."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, with its chain of transmission from Thuwayr ibn Abi Fakhtah, from Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him), who said: "He was asked about the two blasts [of the trumpet]: how much is between them? He said: 'Whatever He wills.'"

Then he (peace be upon him) mentioned the manner of the blast and the death of the inhabitants of the earth and heaven — until he said — "They remain in that state for as long as Allah wills. Then He commands the heaven and it heaves, and He commands the mountains and they move — and that is His saying: يَوْمَ تَمُورُ السَّماءُ مَوْراً وَتَسِيرُ الْجِبالُ سَيْراً The day when the sky will heave with a [terrible] heaving, and the mountains will move with a [terrible] moving. (52:9-10). That is, it is spread out and تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ the earth is turned into another earth (14:48) — meaning an earth upon which sins have not been earned — manifest, with no mountains or vegetation upon it, just as He spread it the first time. And He returns His Throne upon the water as it was the first time — independent in His greatness and power."

He said: "At that point, the Compeller (الجبار), majestic is His majesty, calls out with a loud voice from His side, which is heard in the regions of the heavens and the earths: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ 'To whom does the sovereignty belong today?' But no one answers Him. At that point, the Compeller, the Mighty and Majestic, says, answering Himself: لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ 'To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!'" the narration.

I say: Reflection on the last three narrations guides to the fact that what is annihilated of the creation is the independence of their existence, their relations, and the links of causality between them, as conveyed by the Quranic verses. The spirits do not die, and there is no time between the two blasts, so do not be heedless. In the narrations are subtle indications that appear to the reflective mind, and among them are those whose outward meaning differs from what preceded.

In Rawdat al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), in a narration where he said: "O Abu Ahmad, there is no believer who commits a sin except that it distresses him and he regrets it. The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: 'Regret suffices as repentance,' and he said: 'Whoever is pleased by his good deed and distressed by his evil deed is a believer.' So if he does not regret a sin he commits, he is not a believer, and intercession is not mandatory for him, and he is a wrongdoer (ظالماً). And Allah the Exalted says: مَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ The wrongdoers will have no sympathetic friend, nor any intercessor who might be heard. (40:18)."

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of transmission to Abd al-Rahman ibn Salamah al-Hariri, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يَعْلَمُ خَائِنَةَ الْأَعْيُنِ 'He knows the treachery of the eyes.' He said: 'Have you not seen a man looking at something as if he is not looking? That is the treachery of the eyes.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Abu Dawud, al-Nasa'i, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Sa'd, who said: "On the day of the conquest of Mecca, the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) granted amnesty to the people except for four men and two women, and he said: 'Kill them even if you find them clinging to the curtains of the Kaaba.' Among them was Abdullah ibn Sa'd ibn Abi Sarh, who hid with Uthman ibn Affan."

"When the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) called the people to the pledge of allegiance, he [Uthman] brought him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, accept the pledge of Abdullah.' He looked at him three times, each time refusing to accept his pledge. Then he accepted his pledge, then turned to his companions and said: 'Was there not among you a right-minded man who would stand up to this one when he saw me withholding my hand from his pledge and kill him?' They said: 'We did not know, O Messenger of Allah, what was in your soul. Why did you not signal to us with your eye?' He said: 'It is not befitting for a prophet to have treachery of the eyes (خائنة الأعين).'"

Ayahs

۞ أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَانُوا۟ هُمْ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَءَاثَارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ (٢١)
Have they not travelled through the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? They were greater than them in might and with respect to the effects [they left] in the land. But then Allah seized them for their sins, and they had no defender against Allah [’s punishment]. (21)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانَت تَّأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَكَفَرُوا۟ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ قَوِىٌّ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٢٢)
That was because their apostles used to bring them manifest proofs, but they defied [them]. So Allah seized them. Indeed He is all-strong, severe in retribution. (22)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٣)
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs and a manifest authority (23)
إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَقَـٰرُونَ فَقَالُوا۟ سَـٰحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ (٢٤)
to Pharaoh, Haman and Korah, but they said, ‘A magician and a mendacious liar.’ (24)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ مِنْ عِندِنَا قَالُوا۟ ٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَبْنَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ وَٱسْتَحْيُوا۟ نِسَآءَهُمْ ۚ وَمَا كَيْدُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ (٢٥)
So when he brought them the truth from Us, they said, ‘Kill the sons of the faithful who are with him, and spare their women.’ But the stratagems of the faithless only go awry. (25)
وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ذَرُونِىٓ أَقْتُلْ مُوسَىٰ وَلْيَدْعُ رَبَّهُۥٓ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُبَدِّلَ دِينَكُمْ أَوْ أَن يُظْهِرَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْفَسَادَ (٢٦)
And Pharaoh said, ‘Let me slay Moses, and let him invoke his Lord. Indeed I fear that he will change your religion, or bring forth corruption in our land.’ (26)
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّى عُذْتُ بِرَبِّى وَرَبِّكُم مِّن كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٢٧)
Moses said, ‘I seek the protection of my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Reckoning.’ (27)
وَقَالَ رَجُلٌ مُّؤْمِنٌ مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَكْتُمُ إِيمَـٰنَهُۥٓ أَتَقْتُلُونَ رَجُلًا أَن يَقُولَ رَبِّىَ ٱللَّهُ وَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَكُ كَـٰذِبًا فَعَلَيْهِ كَذِبُهُۥ ۖ وَإِن يَكُ صَادِقًا يُصِبْكُم بَعْضُ ٱلَّذِى يَعِدُكُمْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ (٢٨)
Said a man of faith from Pharaoh’s clan, who concealed his faith, ‘Will you kill a man for saying, ‘‘My Lord is Allah,’’ while he has already brought you manifest proofs from your Lord? Should he be lying, his falsehood will be to his own detriment; but if he is truthful, there shall visit you some of what he promises you. Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a transgressor and liar. (28)
يَـٰقَوْمِ لَكُمُ ٱلْمُلْكُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ظَـٰهِرِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَمَن يَنصُرُنَا مِنۢ بَأْسِ ٱللَّهِ إِن جَآءَنَا ۚ قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ مَآ أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَرَىٰ وَمَآ أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ ٱلرَّشَادِ (٢٩)
O my people! Today sovereignty belongs to you, and you are dominant in the land. But who will save us from Allah’s punishment should it overtake us?’ Pharaoh said, ‘I just point out to you what I see [to be advisable for you], and I guide you only to the way of rectitude.’ (29)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُم مِّثْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ (٣٠)
He who had faith said, ‘O my people! Indeed I fear for you [a day] like the day of the [heathen] factions; (30)
مِثْلَ دَأْبِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ وَثَمُودَ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۚ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِّلْعِبَادِ (٣١)
like the case of the people of Noah, of Ad and Thamud, and those who came after them, and Allah does not desire any wrong for [His] servants. (31)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلتَّنَادِ (٣٢)
O my people! I fear for you a day of mutual distress calls, (32)
يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ مُدْبِرِينَ مَا لَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ ۗ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ (٣٣)
a day when you will turn back [to flee], not having anyone to protect you from Allah, and whomever Allah leads astray has no guide. (33)
وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكُمْ يُوسُفُ مِن قَبْلُ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَمَا زِلْتُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا جَآءَكُم بِهِۦ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا هَلَكَ قُلْتُمْ لَن يَبْعَثَ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ رَسُولًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ مُّرْتَابٌ (٣٤)
Certainly Joseph brought you manifest proofs earlier, but you continued to remain in doubt concerning what he had brought you. When he died, you said, ‘‘Allah will never send any apostle after him.’’ That is how Allah leads astray those who are unrestrained and skeptical. (34)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَـٰنٍ أَتَىٰهُمْ ۖ كَبُرَ مَقْتًا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعِندَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَطْبَعُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ قَلْبِ مُتَكَبِّرٍ جَبَّارٍ (٣٥)
Those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that may have come to them—[that is] greatly outrageous to Allah and to those who have faith. That is how Allah seals the heart of every arrogant tyrant.’ (35)
وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يَـٰهَـٰمَـٰنُ ٱبْنِ لِى صَرْحًا لَّعَلِّىٓ أَبْلُغُ ٱلْأَسْبَـٰبَ (٣٦)
Pharaoh said, ‘O Haman! Build me a tower so that I may reach the routes (36)
أَسْبَـٰبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ فَأَطَّلِعَ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِ مُوسَىٰ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّهُۥ كَـٰذِبًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِفِرْعَوْنَ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ وَصُدَّ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۚ وَمَا كَيْدُ فِرْعَوْنَ إِلَّا فِى تَبَابٍ (٣٧)
—the routes of the heavens—and take a look at the God of Moses, and indeed I consider him a liar.’ The evil of his conduct was thus presented as decorous to Pharaoh, and he was kept from the way [of Allah]. Pharaoh’s stratagems only led him into ruin. (37)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ ٱلرَّشَادِ (٣٨)
And he who had faith said, ‘O my people! Follow me; I will guide you to the way of rectitude. (38)
يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّمَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا مَتَـٰعٌ وَإِنَّ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ هِىَ دَارُ ٱلْقَرَارِ (٣٩)
O my people! This life of the world is only a [passing] enjoyment, and indeed the Hereafter is the abiding home. (39)
مَنْ عَمِلَ سَيِّئَةً فَلَا يُجْزَىٰٓ إِلَّا مِثْلَهَا ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيهَا بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٤٠)
Whoever commits a misdeed shall not be requited except with its like, but whoever acts righteously, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise, and they will be provided therein without any reckoning. (40)
۞ وَيَـٰقَوْمِ مَا لِىٓ أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى ٱلنَّجَوٰةِ وَتَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ (٤١)
O my people! [Think,] what makes me invite you to deliverance, while you invite me toward the Fire? (41)
تَدْعُونَنِى لِأَكْفُرَ بِٱللَّهِ وَأُشْرِكَ بِهِۦ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ وَأَنَا۠ أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرِ (٤٢)
You invite me to defy Allah and to ascribe to Him partners of which I have no knowledge, while I call you to the All-mighty, the All-forgiver. (42)
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّمَا تَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَيْهِ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ دَعْوَةٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَلَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَنَّ مَرَدَّنَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَأَنَّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ هُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ (٤٣)
Undoubtedly, that to which you invite me has no invitation in the world nor in the Hereafter, and indeed our return will be to Allah, and indeed it is the transgressors who will be inmates of the Fire. (43)
فَسَتَذْكُرُونَ مَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ ۚ وَأُفَوِّضُ أَمْرِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (٤٤)
