Soon you will remember what I tell you, and I entrust my affair to Allah. Indeed Allah watches His servants.’ (44)
فَوَقَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِ مَا مَكَرُوا۟ ۖ وَحَاقَ بِـَٔالِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوٓءُ ٱلْعَذَابِ (٤٥)
Then Allah saved him from their evil schemes, while a terrible punishment besieged Pharaoh’s clan: (45)
ٱلنَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا ۖ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوٓا۟ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ ٱلْعَذَابِ (٤٦)
the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening. On the day when the Hour sets in, Pharaoh’s clan will enter the severest punishment. (46)
وَإِذْ يَتَحَآجُّونَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ فَيَقُولُ ٱلضُّعَفَـٰٓؤُا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا لَكُمْ تَبَعًا فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّغْنُونَ عَنَّا نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ (٤٧)
When they argue in the Fire, the oppressed will say to the oppressors, ‘We used to follow you; will you avail us against any portion of the Fire?’ (47)
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُلٌّ فِيهَآ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ حَكَمَ بَيْنَ ٱلْعِبَادِ (٤٨)
The oppressors will say, ‘We are all in it [together]; Allah has judged between [His] servants.’ (48)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ لِخَزَنَةِ جَهَنَّمَ ٱدْعُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ يُخَفِّفْ عَنَّا يَوْمًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ (٤٩)
Those who are in the Fire will say to the keepers of hell, ‘Invoke your Lord to lighten for us [at least] a day’s punishment.’ (49)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَوَلَمْ تَكُ تَأْتِيكُمْ رُسُلُكُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ ۚ قَالُوا۟ فَٱدْعُوا۟ ۗ وَمَا دُعَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ (٥٠)
They will say, ‘Had not your apostles brought you manifest proofs?’ They will say, ‘Yes.’ They will say, ‘Then invoke [Him] yourselves.’ But the invocations of the faithless only go awry. (50)
إِنَّا لَنَنصُرُ رُسُلَنَا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلْأَشْهَـٰدُ (٥١)
Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up, (51)
يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مَعْذِرَتُهُمْ ۖ وَلَهُمُ ٱللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلدَّارِ (٥٢)
the day when the excuses of the wrongdoers will not benefit them, the curse will lie on them, and for them will be the ills of the [ultimate] abode. (52)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَأَوْرَثْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ (٥٣)
Certainly We gave guidance to Moses and We gave the Children of Israel the Book as a legacy, (53)
هُدًى وَذِكْرَىٰ لِأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٥٤)
as guidance and admonition for those who possess intellect. (54)

(Commentary)

In these verses, there is an admonition for them by referring back to the remnants of past nations and their stories for observation and reflection. They should look into them and take heed, realizing that Allah, the Glorified, is not frustrated by the power of the powerful, the arrogance of the arrogant, or the plotting of the plotters. The text mentions, as a representative sample, a portion of the stories of Moses and Pharaoh, including the story of the Believer from the Family of Pharaoh.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا — "Have they not travelled in the land and seen..." (40:21) to the end of the verse. The interrogation here is for denial (إنكاري). The word الواقي (protector) is the active participle from protection (الوقاية) — the act of preserving a thing from what harms or injures it.

The meaning is: Have these people to whom We sent you not traveled "in the land and seen" (فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا) with a gaze of contemplation and reflection "how was the fate of those who were before them" (كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ) among the bygone nations who denied their messengers? "They were greater than them in might" (كانُوا هُمْ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً), meaning in power, capability, and authority, "and effects" (وَآثاراً) such as fortified cities, impregnable fortresses, and lofty, well-built palaces "in the land. So Allah seized them for their sins" (فِي الْأَرْضِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ) and destroyed them because of their deeds, "and they had no protector from Allah" (وَما كانَ لَهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ واقٍ) to shield them or any guardian to preserve them.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كانَتْ تَأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ — "That was because their apostles used to come to them with manifest proofs..." (40:22) etc. The word "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to the divine seizure. What is meant by manifest proofs (البينات) are the clear signs (الآيات الواضحات), and the rest of the verse is self-evident.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا مُوسى بِآياتِنا وَسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ — "Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs and a manifest authority" (40:23). Perhaps what is meant by the signs (الآيات) are the miraculous wonders (الخوارق المعجزة) with which he was sent, such as the staff and the hand and others; and by manifest authority (السلطان المبين), the overwhelming divine power (السلطة الإلهية القاهرة) with which he was supported, which prevented Pharaoh from killing him and extinguishing his light. It has also been said that the signs refer to arguments and evidences, while the authority refers to his miracles like the staff and the hand; other views have also been mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ وَهامانَ وَقارُونَ فَقالُوا ساحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ — "to Pharaoh, Haman and Korah. But they said, ‘[He is] a magician, a mendacious liar.’" (40:24). Pharaoh was the tyrant of the Copts and their king; Haman was his minister; and Korah was one of the transgressors among the Children of Israel, the owner of treasure-houses filled with wealth.

These three were specifically mentioned from among the two peoples because they were the foundations (أصولا) to which every corruption and discord (فتنة) within them traced back.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ مِنْ عِنْدِنا قالُوا اقْتُلُوا أَبْناءَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ — "So when he brought them the truth from Us, they said, ‘Kill the sons of those who have believed with him...’" (40:25) etc. This is a comparison between what Moses brought them and called them toward, and what they countered him with of their plotting (كيد). He brought them the truth (الحق), and it was obligatory for them to accept it because it was truth; and what he brought was from Allah (مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ), so it was obligatory for them to accept it and not reject it. Yet they countered it with plotting and said what they said so that no one would believe in him. However, Allah made their plot go astray, so it did not harm the believers with him.

The context suggests that among those who spoke these words was Korah, even though he was from the Children of Israel. There is no contradiction in this, for the decree to kill the sons and spare the women had been issued against the Children of Israel in general before the mission (الدعوة); this decree, however, was specifically against the believers among them. Perhaps Korah agreed with them on this due to his enmity and hatred toward Moses and the believers of his people.

In His saying: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ — "those who have believed with him," rather than saying "believed in him," there is an indication of their public support for Moses in his mission.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ذَرُونِي أَقْتُلْ مُوسى وَلْيَدْعُ رَبَّهُ — "Pharaoh said, ‘Let me kill Moses, and let him call his Lord...’" (40:26) etc. "Let me" (ذَرُونِي) means leave me; it is an address to his assembly (ملأه). This indicates that there were people advising him not to kill Moses and to refrain from him, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا أَرْجِهْ وَأَخاهُ — "They said, ‘Put him and his brother off...’" (26:36).

His saying: وَلْيَدْعُ رَبَّهُ — "and let him call his Lord" is a statement he made out of arrogance and rebellion (عتوا). He is saying: Leave me to kill him, and let him call his Lord so that He may save him from my hand and rescue him from death, if He is able.

His saying: إِنِّي أَخافُ أَنْ يُبَدِّلَ دِينَكُمْ أَوْ أَنْ يُظْهِرَ فِي الْأَرْضِ الْفَسادَ — "‘...Indeed I fear that he will change your religion, or cause corruption to appear in the land.’" (40:26). This is the justification for his intent to kill. He mentioned that he feared for them regarding their religion and their worldly life. As for their religion—which was the worship of idols—he feared he would change it and replace it with the worship of Allah alone. As for their worldly life, he feared his matter would become great, his side would grow strong, and his followers would increase, leading them to manifest rebellion and opposition, which would result in strife, fighting, and the loss of security.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ مُوسى إِنِّي عُذْتُ بِرَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ الْحِسابِ — "Moses said, ‘Indeed I seek the protection of my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Reckoning.’" (40:27). This is a counter-response from him (peace be upon him) to Pharaoh's threat of killing him, and a seeking of refuge in his Lord. His saying: عُذْتُ بِرَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ — "I seek the protection of my Lord and your Lord" is also a counter to Pharaoh's saying: وَلْيَدْعُ رَبَّهُ — "and let him call his Lord," where Pharaoh restricted the Lordship (الربوبية) of the Exalted to Moses. Thus, Moses indicated by his saying "my Lord and your Lord" that the Exalted is their Lord just as He is his Lord, and His decree is effective over them just as it is effective over him; therefore, He has the power to protect the one who seeks His refuge from their evil—and He did indeed protect him.

From this, it becomes clear that the address in His saying: وَرَبِّكُمْ — "and your Lord" is to Pharaoh and those with him, rather than his own people from the Children of Israel.

His saying: مِنْ كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ الْحِسابِ — "from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Reckoning" refers to Pharaoh and everyone who shares these two attributes: arrogance and lack of belief in the Day of Reckoning. No evil is safe from one in whom these two traits are combined.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ رَجُلٌ مُؤْمِنٌ مِنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَكْتُمُ إِيمانَهُ — "A faithful man from Pharaoh’s clan, who concealed his faith, said..." (40:28) to the end of the verse.

The apparent context is that "from Pharaoh’s clan" (مِنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ) is a description of the man, and "who concealed his faith" (يَكْتُمُ إِيمانَهُ) is another description. Thus, the man was from the Copts, among the inner circle of Pharaoh, and they did not know of his faith because he concealed it from them out of precautionary dissimulation (التقية).

It has been said that His saying: "from Pharaoh’s clan" is the second object of His saying: "concealed" (يَكْتُمُ), placed before it. Although the verb usually takes the second object directly, as in His saying: وَلا يَكْتُمُونَ اللهَ حَدِيثاً — "and they will not conceal any matter from Allah" (4:42), it can also take it via the preposition "from" (من), as explicitly stated in al-Misbah.

However, the context rejects this, as there is no apparent rhetorical point—such as restriction (حصر) or the like—necessitating the placement of the second object before the verb. Furthermore, the man repeatedly addresses Pharaoh and his people with the term "O my people" (يا قَوْمِ); had he not been one of them, he would not have been entitled to do so.

His saying: أَتَقْتُلُونَ رَجُلاً أَنْ يَقُولَ رَبِّيَ اللهُ وَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ — "‘Will you kill a man for saying, “My Lord is Allah,” while he has already brought you manifest proofs from your Lord?’" (40:28). This is a denunciation of their intent to kill him. In His saying: "from your Lord" (مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ), there is proof that the manifest proofs he brought contained evidence that Allah is their Lord as well, just as he had taken Him as a Lord. Thus, killing him would be the killing of a man who brought the truth from their own Lord.

His saying: وَإِنْ يَكُ كاذِباً فَعَلَيْهِ كَذِبُهُ — "‘If he is a liar, his lying will be to his own detriment...’" (40:28). It is said that his mentioning of this possibility was a form of diplomacy (تلطف) on his part, not that he was in doubt regarding Moses' truthfulness.

His saying: وَإِنْ يَكُ صادِقاً يُصِبْكُمْ بَعْضُ الَّذِي يَعِدُكُمْ — "‘...and if he is truthful, some of what he promises you will strike you.’" (40:28). This is a form of concession in argumentation (تنزل في المخاصمة) by sufficing with the simplest and least of possibilities. It is as if he is saying: If he is truthful, what he promised you of various types of punishment will strike you—or at the very least, some of what he promises will strike you, even though his truthfulness necessitates that all he promised will occur.

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ — "‘...Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a profligate, a liar.’" (40:28). This is a justification for the second possibility only. The meaning is: If he is a liar, his lie suffices against him; but if he is truthful, some of what he promises will strike you, because you would then be profligates (مسرفون)—transgressing your bounds—and liars (كذابون) in denying the Lordship of your Lord and taking lords besides Him. And Allah does not guide one who is a profligate and a liar. As for the possibility of his being a liar, there is no Lordship for the one he took as a Lord such that He would guide him or not guide him.

From this, it appears that what some have mentioned—that this sentence is a justification for both possibilities and relates to both clauses—is not sound.

His saying, the Exalted: يا قَوْمِ لَكُمُ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ ظاهِرِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَمَنْ يَنْصُرُنا مِنْ بَأْسِ اللهِ إِنْ جاءَنا — "‘O my people! Today the kingdom is yours, as you are dominant in the land. But who will help us against Allah’s punishment should it come to us?’" (40:29). Their dominance (ظهورهم) is their victory and superiority in the land; the land refers to the land of Egypt. The "punishment of Allah" (بأس الله) is His seizure and chastisement. The interrogation is for denial.

The meaning is: O my people, the kingdom is yours today while you are dominant and superior in the land of Egypt over those beneath you among the Children of Israel; so who will help us against Allah's seizure and punishment—as Moses promises us—should it come to us? He included himself among them regarding the possibility of the punishment's arrival to be more effective in his sincere advice and to strike their hearts with the sense that he desires for them the same safety he desires for himself.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ما أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا ما أَرى وَما أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ — "Pharaoh said, ‘I only show you what I see [to be right], and I only guide you to the way of rectitude.’" (40:29). That is, the path of correctness that corresponds to reality. He intends that he is certain of the path to which he guides his people, and that it is both known to him and corresponding to reality. This was a deception (تمويه) and a display of false fortitude (تجلد) on his part.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِي آمَنَ يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ مِثْلَ يَوْمِ الْأَحْزابِ — "He who had believed said, ‘O my people! Indeed I fear for you the like of the day of the factions...’" (40:30) to His saying: لِلْعِبادِ — "...for [His] servants" (40:31). The one who believed is the Believer from the Family of Pharaoh. No weight is given to the claim that it was Moses, despite the strength of the speech. The "factions" (الأحزاب) refer to the nations mentioned in the following verse: the people of Noah, ‘Ad, Thamud, and those after them. His saying: مِثْلَ دَأْبِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ — "the like of the case of the people of Noah" (40:31) is an explanation of the previous "like" (مثل). The word الدأب (case/custom) means habit or practice.

The meaning is: O my people, I fear for you the like of the day of past nations—the like of the ongoing custom of punishment upon them, one after another, for their disbelief and denial of the messengers; or the like of the recompense for their persistent habit of disbelief and denial. And Allah does not desire injustice for His servants.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيا قَوْمِ إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ يَوْمَ التَّنادِ — "‘O my people! Indeed I fear for you the Day of Calling...’" (40:32) to His saying: مِنْ هادٍ — "...any guide" (40:33). The "Day of Calling" (يَوْمَ التَّنادِ) is the Day of Resurrection. Perhaps it is named so because the wrongdoers therein will call out to one another, and they will cry out with "woe" and "destruction" as was their habit in the world.

It has been said that the "calling" refers to the call that occurs between the inhabitants of Paradise and the inhabitants of the Fire, as mentioned by Allah the Exalted in Surah al-A‘raf. There are other views mentioned which are of no benefit.

His saying: يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ مُدْبِرِينَ ما لَكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ عاصِمٍ — "the day you will turn to flee, with no one to protect you from Allah" (40:33). This refers to the Day of Resurrection. Perhaps it means they will flee within the Fire from the intensity of its punishment to escape it, but they will be returned to it, as the Exalted said: كُلَّما أَرادُوا أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها مِنْ غَمٍّ أُعِيدُوا فِيها وَذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ — "Whenever they desire to leave it out of anguish, they will be turned back into it [and told]: ‘Taste the punishment of the burning!’" (22:22).

His saying: وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ — "and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide" (40:33) is like a justification for His saying: "with no one to protect you from Allah." That is, you flee turning your backs, having no protector; and if there were to be one, it would have to be from Allah's side, but there is none. This is because Allah has led them astray, and whomever Allah leads astray has no guide.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ يُوسُفُ مِنْ قَبْلُ بِالْبَيِّناتِ — "Certainly Joseph brought you manifest proofs before..." (40:34) to the end of the verse. Since he mentioned that Allah led them astray and they have no guide, he cited as evidence their treatment of Joseph (peace be upon him) regarding his message to them, as they doubted his prophethood as long as he was alive, then when he died, they said: "No prophet shall come after him."

The meaning is: I swear, Joseph brought you before this the manifest signs that leave no doubt regarding his message from Allah, yet you remained in doubt regarding what he brought you as long as he was alive, until when he perished and died, you said: "Allah will never send a messenger after him." Thus you contradicted yourselves and did not care.

Then He emphasized this—and it carries the meaning of a justification—by His saying: كَذلِكَ يُضِلُّ اللهُ مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ مُرْتابٌ — "Thus does Allah lead astray those who are profligate and skeptics" (40:34).

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِ اللهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطانٍ أَتاهُمْ — "Those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that may have come to them..." (40:35) etc. This is a description of every profligate skeptic. For whoever transgresses his bounds by turning away from the truth and following whims, and skepticism becomes settled in his soul such that he does not settle upon knowledge nor find peace in an argument that guides him to the truth, will dispute the signs of Allah without proof whenever they oppose the dictates of his whims.

His saying: كَذلِكَ يَطْبَعُ اللهُ عَلى كُلِّ قَلْبِ مُتَكَبِّرٍ جَبَّارٍ — "Thus does Allah seal every arrogant tyrant’s heart" (40:35) indicates that their hearts are sealed, so they do not comprehend an argument nor rely upon a proof.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يا هامانُ ابْنِ لِي صَرْحاً — "Pharaoh said, ‘O Haman! Build me a tower...’" (40:36) to His saying: فِي تَبابٍ — "...in ruin" (40:37). This is an order from him to his minister Haman to build him a structure through which he might reach a way to see the God of Moses. Perhaps he issued this order during the debate with the Believer and after turning away from killing Moses; therefore, it was mentioned between the admonitions and arguments of the Believer.

The صرح (tower/lofty palace)—according to al-Majma‘—is a prominent building that is not hidden from the eye of the observer even from a distance. The أسباب (means/ways) is the plural of sabab, which is that by which you reach what is distant from you.

His saying: لَعَلِّي أَبْلُغُ الْأَسْبابَ — "‘...that I may reach the routes’" (40:36) is in the sense of a justification for his order to build the tower. The meaning is: I order you to build it because I hope that by ascending it I may reach the routes. Then he explained the routes by His saying: أَسْبابَ السَّماواتِ — "‘the routes of the heavens’" (40:36) and branched from it His saying: فَأَطَّلِعَ إِلى إِلهِ مُوسى — "‘and take a look at the God of Moses’" (40:37). It is as if he is saying: The God whom Moses calls upon and invites to is not on earth, for there is no god on it besides me; so perhaps He is in the heaven. Therefore, build me a tower so that I may reach, by ascending it, the heavenly routes that reveal the secrets of the heaven, and thus I may look from that direction toward the God of Moses—"though I indeed consider him a liar" (40:37).

It has been said that his intention was to build an observatory to monitor heavenly conditions, hoping to find therein something to prove the existence of Moses' God after despairing of finding Him through earthly means. This is a fine interpretation. In any case, what he mentioned does not align with any of the doctrines of idolatry (الوثنية); perhaps it was a deception of the people or due to his own ignorance—and such is not far-fetched for the wrongdoers.

His saying: وَكَذلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِفِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ وَصُدَّ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ — "Thus his evil conduct was made to seem decorous to Pharaoh, and he was barred from the [right] way" (40:37). The sense of the context is that this provides a general rule for how Pharaoh confronted the truth to which Moses invited him. Satan made his ugly deed seem beautiful to him, so he saw it as good; and he was barred from the way of rectitude, yet he saw his being barred as a pursuit of it. Thus he disputed the signs of Allah with falsehood and performed such ugly deeds and foolish plots to refute the truth.

Therefore, the verse concluded with His saying: وَما كَيْدُ فِرْعَوْنَ إِلَّا فِي تَبابٍ — "and Pharaoh’s stratagem was only in ruin" (40:37), meaning destruction and loss.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِي آمَنَ يا قَوْمِ اتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ — "He who had believed said, ‘O my people! Follow me, I will guide you to the way of rectitude.’" (40:38). He invites them to follow him so that he may guide them; following him is following Moses. The "way of rectitude" (سبيل الرشاد) is the path in whose traversal lies the attainment of truth and the achievement of felicity. Guidance (الهداية) here is in the sense of showing the way. In His saying: "I will guide you to the way of rectitude," there is a subtle rebuke to Pharaoh, who had said: "I only guide you to the way of rectitude." The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ مِثْلَ يَوْمِ الْأَحْزابِ — "‘O my people! This life of the world is only a passing enjoyment, and the Hereafter is indeed the abiding home.’" (40:39). This is the foundation (السناد) upon which the traversal of the way of rectitude and the practice of the true religion rests; it is indispensable in any case. It is the belief that for man there is an eternal, everlasting life, which is the life of the Hereafter, and that this worldly life is a "passing enjoyment" (متاع) in relation to the Hereafter and a means intended for its sake. Therefore, he began with this in explaining the way of rectitude, then mentioned the evil deed and the righteous deed.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ عَمِلَ سَيِّئَةً فَلا يُجْزى إِلَّا مِثْلَها — "Whoever commits a misdeed shall not be requited except with its like..." (40:40) to the end of the verse. That is, what will strike him and what he will live by in the Hereafter will resemble what he brought forth in this worldly life, which is a passing enjoyment therein. For the world is the abode of action, and the Hereafter is the abode of recompense.

Whoever performs an evil deed (سيئة)—characterized by badness—in the world shall not be requited in the Hereafter except with its like from what will grieve him. And whoever performs a righteous deed (صالحا), whether male or female—there being no difference between them in this—while being a believer, those shall enter Paradise, being provided therein without stint.

In this, there is an indication of the equality between male and female in the acceptance of deeds, and the conditioning of the righteous deed's effect upon faith (الإيمان), because a deed is nullified (حبطا) without faith. The Exalted said: وَمَنْ يَكُفُرْ بِالْإِيمانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ — "and should anyone renounce the faith, his work shall fail" (5:5), among other verses.

The true religion, which is the way of rectitude, has been summarized in the briefest explanation: that for man there is an abiding home (دار قرار) wherein he is requited for what he did in the world of evil or righteous deeds; so let him do righteousness and not do evil. He added further clarification by stating that if he does righteousness, he will be provided for without stint.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيا قَوْمِ ما لِي أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى النَّجاةِ وَتَدْعُونَنِي إِلَى النَّارِ — "‘O my people! How is it that I call you to salvation, while you call me to the Fire?’" (40:41) to His saying: الْعَزِيزِ الْغَفَّارِ — "...the All-mighty, the All-forgiver" (40:42). It is as if when he called them to monotheism (التوحيد), they countered him by calling him to the worship of their gods, or he presumed this of them when he witnessed their disputation with falsehood and their persistence in polytheism (الشرك). Thus, he attributed the invitation to them based on the testimony of their state, expressing wonder at their countering his true invitation with their false one.

He said: "O my people! How is it that I call you to salvation"—meaning salvation from the Fire—"while you call me to the Fire?" He was calling them to the cause of salvation, while they were calling him to the cause of entering the Fire; thus he equated the invitation to the two causes with an invitation to the two effects, or because the recompense is the deed itself in a certain respect.

Then he explained what they called him to and what he called them to, saying: تَدْعُونَنِي لِأَكْفُرَ — "‘You call me to defy’" (40:42), meaning to disbelieve "in Allah and to ascribe to Him partners of which I have no knowledge" (بِاللهِ وَأُشْرِكَ بِهِ ما لَيْسَ لِي بِهِ عِلْمٌ). That is, to associate with Him something for which I have no proof of its being a partner, thus fabricating against Allah without knowledge. "While I call you to the All-mighty" (وَأَنَا أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى الْعَزِيزِ)—the One Who overcomes and is not overcome—"the All-forgiver" (الْغَفَّارِ) to whoever turns to Him and believes in Him; meaning, I call you to faith in Him and submission (الإسلام) to Him.

His saying, the Exalted: لا جَرَمَ أَنَّما تَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ لَيْسَ لَهُ دَعْوَةٌ فِي الدُّنْيا وَلا فِي الْآخِرَةِ — "Undoubtedly, what you call me to has no title to be invoked in this world or the Hereafter..." (40:43) etc. "Undoubtedly" (لا جَرَمَ) means truly or necessarily. The sense of the verse is the establishment of proof for the non-existence of the divinity of what they call to, by way of the absence of any invitation (دعوة) to it. In this, there is support for his saying in the previous verse: "of which I have no knowledge."

The meaning is: It is firmly established that what you call me to, which you name as a partner to Him, the Glorified, has no invitation in the world, as no prophet is known to have been sent to people from its side to call them to its worship; nor in the Hereafter, as no one returns to it therein. As for the One I call you to, who is Allah the Glorified, He indeed has an invitation in the world, which is that undertaken by His prophets and messengers sent from Him, supported by arguments and manifest proofs; and in the Hereafter, which is followed by the return of creation to Him for the final judgment between them. The Exalted said: يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِ — "The day He will call you, and you will respond with His praise" (17:52).

It is well known, as we established in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمْ آياتِهِ — "It is He who shows you His signs" (40:13), that Lordship is not complete without an invitation in the world, and its counterpart is the invitation in the Hereafter. Since the One he calls them to possesses an invitation in the world and the Hereafter, while what they call him to does not, He is the God, not what they call to.

His saying: وَأَنَّ مَرَدَّنا إِلَى اللهِ وَأَنَّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ هُمْ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ — "‘...and that our return is to Allah, and that the profligates are the inmates of the Fire’" (40:43) is conjoined to His saying: "that what you call me to." That is: undoubtedly our return is to Allah, so submission to Him, following His path, and observing the limits of servitude (العبودية) are mandatory. And undoubtedly the profligates—those who transgress the bounds of servitude, who are you—are the inmates of the Fire. Thus, that to which I call you contains salvation, unlike that to which you call me.

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَتَذْكُرُونَ ما أَقُولُ لَكُمْ وَأُفَوِّضُ أَمْرِي إِلَى اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ بَصِيرٌ بِالْعِبادِ — "‘Soon you will remember what I tell you, and I entrust my affair to Allah. Indeed Allah is watchful of [His] servants.’" (40:44). The beginning of the verse is an admonition and a warning to them, and it is a branch of His saying: "and that our return is to Allah" etc. That is: since the return to Allah and the descent of punishment upon the profligates—of whom you are—is inevitable, and you have not listened today to what I say to you, you will soon remember what I tell you when you witness the punishment, and you will know then that I was a sincere advisor to you.

His saying: وَأُفَوِّضُ أَمْرِي إِلَى اللهِ — "and I entrust my affair to Allah." Entrustment (التفويض)—as explained by al-Raghib—is the act of returning (الرد). Thus, entrusting the affair to Allah is returning it to Him. It is close in meaning to reliance (التوكل) and submission (التسليم), though the perspective differs: Entrustment (التفويض) from the servant is his returning what is attributed to him of the affair to Allah the Glorified; the state of the servant then is that of one who is unarmed, with no affair returning to him. Reliance (التوكل) from the servant is his making his Lord a trustee (وكيلا) to act in what belongs to him of the affair. Submission (التسليم) from the servant is his pure compliance with what Allah the Glorified desires in him and from him, without regard to the attribution of any affair to himself. These are three stations among the stations of servitude: reliance, then entrustment—which is more subtle than reliance—then submission, which is more subtle than both.

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ بَصِيرٌ بِالْعِبادِ — "Indeed Allah is watchful of [His] servants" is the justification for his entrusting his affair to Allah. The use of the Majestic Name (اسم الجلالة) in place of its pronoun—where the apparent context would have required a pronoun—is an indication of the cause of His watchfulness over the servants; it is as if it were said: He is watchful of the servants because He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

His saying, the Exalted: فَوَقاهُ اللهُ سَيِّئاتِ ما مَكَرُوا — "So Allah saved him from the evils of their stratagems..." (40:45). This is a branch of his entrusting the affair to Allah; thus Allah sufficed him against their evil and protected him from the evils of their plotting. In this, there is an indication that they intended evil for him, but Allah repelled them from him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَحاقَ بِآلِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ الْعَذابِ — "and a terrible punishment beset Pharaoh’s clan" (40:45) to His saying: أَشَدَّ الْعَذابِ — "...the severest punishment" (40:46). That is, the evil punishment descended upon them and struck them. "Evil punishment" (سوء العذاب) is the addition of an attribute to its described noun, and the description with the infinitive (المصدر) implies emphasis. "Pharaoh’s clan" (آل فرعون) refers to his partisans and followers; sometimes "the clan of so-and-so" is said and it includes the person himself.

His saying: النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذابِ — "[It is] the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening, and on the day when the Hour sets in [it will be said], ‘Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment.’" (40:46). The apparent context is that this is an explanation of the "evil punishment" and is not a new sentence (استئناف).

The verse is explicit, firstly, that there is an exposure (عرض) to the Fire and then an admission (إدخال) into it, and admission is more severe than exposure. Secondly, that the exposure to the Fire is before the setting in of the Hour in which the admission occurs; this is the punishment of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection. Thirdly, that the punishment in the intermediate realm and on the day the Hour sets in is by one thing, which is the Fire of the Hereafter, but those in the intermediate realm are punished by it from a distance, while the people of the Hereafter are punished by entering it.

In His saying: غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا — "morning and evening," there is an indication of continuity without interruption. Perhaps for the people of the intermediate realm, because they are not entirely severed from the world, there is some relation to morning and evening.

In His saying: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا — "and on the day when the Hour sets in [it will be said], ‘Admit...’" there is brevity through omission (إيجاز بالحذف); the estimated meaning is: It will be said: Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ يَتَحاجُّونَ فِي النَّارِ فَيَقُولُ الضُّعَفاءُ لِلَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا — "When they wrangle in the Fire, the weak will say to those who were arrogant..." (40:47) to His saying: بَيْنَ الْعِبادِ — "...among [His] servants" (40:48). The context indicates that the pronoun in "they wrangle" (يَتَحاجُّونَ) refers to Pharaoh’s clan. Evidence for this is the change in context in His later saying: "And those who are in the Fire will say..." (40:49). The meaning is: A terrible punishment beset Pharaoh’s clan when they wrangle in the Fire; or: Mention of their terrible punishment when they wrangle in the Fire, and the weak among them say to those who were arrogant: "In the world, we were your followers, and the consequence of that should have been that you suffice us in our needs and help us in hardships. There is no hardship more severe than what we are in; so will you avail us of a portion of the Fire?" Even if it is not all of its punishment, we are content with a part.

This is a manifestation of what was ingrained in their souls in the world of seeking refuge in their arrogant leaders and those they followed instead of Allah. This appears from them on the Day of Resurrection even though they know they are in a day when no soul shall avail another soul in anything, and the command that day belongs to Allah. There are similar instances recounted about them in the speech of the Exalted regarding their lying on that day, their breaking of oaths, their denial of their deeds, and their calling one another liars, among other things.

His saying: قالَ الَّذِي اسْتَكْبَرُوا إِنَّا كُلٌّ فِيها إِنَّ اللهَ قَدْ حَكَمَ بَيْنَ الْعِبادِ — "Those who were arrogant will say, ‘Indeed we are all in it. Indeed Allah has judged between [His] servants.’" (40:48). This is the response of their arrogant ones to their statement. Its summary is that today is a day of recompense, not a day of action; thus causes (الأسباب) have lost their effect, and what we used to imagine for ourselves in the world of power and capability has vanished. So our state and your state—while we are all in the Fire—is one and the same.

Their saying: "Indeed we are all in it. Indeed Allah has judged between [His] servants" means that the manifestation of the divine judgment has nullified the judgments of all other causes and their effects, and has established us in the state we are in on equal footing. Thus, we do not possess any power over you such that we could avail you of any of the punishment.

Among what has been said regarding the verse is that the pronoun in "they wrangle" refers to all disbelievers among the people of the Fire, which is far-fetched as you have seen. It has also been said the pronoun refers to the Quraysh, which is even further fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِي فِي النَّارِ لِخَزَنَةِ جَهَنَّمَ ادْعُوا رَبَّكُمْ يُخَفِّفْ عَنَّا يَوْماً مِنَ الْعَذابِ — "Those who are in the Fire will say to the keepers of hell, ‘Call your Lord to lighten the punishment for us for a day.’" (40:49). This is a conversation between the people of the Fire—among whom is Pharaoh’s clan—and the keepers of Hell, which the Glorified mentioned following the story of Pharaoh’s clan. They only asked the keepers to pray for them because of their despair that they themselves would be answered.

What is meant by a "day" (يَوْماً) of punishment is what is appropriate to the meaning of a day for the world they are in; its meaning boils down to a portion of the punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا أَوَلَمْ تَكُ تَأْتِيكُمْ رُسُلُكُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ قالُوا بَلى قالُوا فَادْعُوا وَما دُعاءُ الْكافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ — "They will say, ‘Did not your apostles use to bring you manifest proofs?’ They will say, ‘Yes.’ They will say, ‘Then do call!’ But the prayer of the faithless only goes astray." (40:50). They answered them by inquiring about their messengers bringing them manifest proofs, and they confessed to that. This is a confession from them that they disbelieved in them despite knowing they were upon the truth, which is disbelief in prophethood. Thus, the keepers did not answer them regarding what they asked of prayer, neither affirmatively nor negatively, but rather referred them back to themselves, indicating that no prayer of theirs would be answered.

His saying: وَما دُعاءُ الْكافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ — "But the prayer of the faithless only goes astray" means that their prayer is encompassed by straying, so it does not find its way to the goal of being answered. This is the completion of the keepers' speech as suggested by the context; it is also possible that it is from the speech of the Exalted, though this is less likely.

In any case, the sentence provides the meaning of a justification. The summary is: Call, but you will not be answered, for you are disbelievers, and the disbelievers' prayer is not answered.

The suspension of the ruling of non-answering upon the attribute of disbelief (الكفر) suggests its causality. This is because Allah the Glorified, although He gave His servants a definitive promise to answer the prayer of whoever calls upon Him among them, saying: أُُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ — "I answer the solicitor’s invocation when he invokes Me" (2:186)—and the prayer, when it truly occurs, is never rejected—yet what is contained within the text of this promise is that there must be a true prayer and request, and that it must truly relate to Allah. That is, the caller must call and request in earnest, and in doing so, must turn exclusively to Allah, away from all other causes that he calls causes.

The one who disbelieves in the punishment of the Hereafter—who denies it and conceals its reality—cannot produce a sincere request for its removal. In the world, this is obvious; as for the Hereafter, although he becomes certain of it by sight and turns exclusively to Allah the Glorified due to the intensity he is in, and causes have been severed from him, yet the attribute of denial has clung to him as a consequence and he has been requited for it; thus it does not let him request what he used to deny with a sincere request.

Furthermore, the discussion regarding his turning exclusively to Allah is like the discussion regarding his sincere request for deliverance: how can he turn exclusively to Allah there when he did not clothe himself with it in the world? So understand this.

By this, the weakness of using the verse as evidence that the disbeliever's prayer is not answered absolutely becomes apparent. For you have seen that the verse's indication is the non-answering of his prayer regarding what he disbelieves in and denies, not absolutely. How could it be otherwise, when there are many verses mentioning the answering of his prayer in cases of necessity (اضطرار)?

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ — "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up" (40:51). The "witnesses" (الأشهاد) is the plural of shahid, meaning witness. The verse is a generic promise, not a personal promise for every single one of them in every single event. A discussion regarding the meaning of divine help (النصر) has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ — "that they will indeed be the ones to be helped" (37:172).

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ الظَّالِمِينَ مَعْذِرَتُهُمْ وَلَهُمُ اللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوءُ الدَّارِ — "the day when the excuses of the wrongdoers will not benefit them, and the curse will be theirs, and the terrible abode will be theirs" (40:52). This is an explanation of "the day when the witnesses rise up." The apparent addition of the infinitive to its subject in His saying "their excuses" (مَعْذِرَتُهُمْ)—rather than saying "if they excuse themselves"—indicates the occurrence of some excuse from them on that day. As for His saying: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ وَلا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ — "This is a day they will not speak, nor will they be permitted to offer excuses" (77:35-36), it is understood as referring to some stages and stations of the Day of Resurrection, because other verses indicate the occurrence of some speech from them on that day.

His saying: وَلَهُمُ اللَّعْنَةُ — "and the curse will be theirs" means distance from the mercy of Allah. His saying: لَهُمْ سُوءُ الدَّارِ — "the terrible abode will be theirs" refers to the evil abode, which is Hell.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْهُدى وَأَوْرَثْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ — "Certainly We gave Moses the guidance and We made the Children of Israel inherit the Book" (40:53) to His saying: الْأَلْبابِ — "...for those who possess intellect" (40:54). This is a conclusion to what preceded regarding the sending of Moses with signs and manifest authority, the disputation of Pharaoh’s clan against the signs with falsehood, and the argument of the Believer from Pharaoh’s clan. He indicates by it—having begun with the "L" of the oath—the truth of what he was sent with and their injustice in how they confronted it.

What is meant by "the guidance" (الهدى) is the religion given to Moses, and by "making the Children of Israel inherit the Book" is the preservation of the Torah among them, by which they act and are guided.

His saying: هُدىً وَذِكْرى لِأُولِي الْأَلْبابِ — "as a guidance and admonition for those who possess intellect" (40:54). That is, while the Book is a guidance (هدى) by which their commonality is guided, and an admonition (ذكرى) by which their elite—those who possess intellect (أولي الألباب)—take heed.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ilal, with its chain of narration from Isma‘il ibn Mansur Abu Ziyad, from a man, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding the saying of Pharaoh: ذَرُونِي أَقْتُلْ مُوسى — "Let me kill Moses," what prevented him? He said: "His sound birth (رشدته) prevented him; and none kill prophets or the children of prophets except those born of adultery."

In al-Majma‘, Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "التقية ديني ودين آبائي ، ولا دين لمن لا تقية له ، والتقية ترس الله في الأرض — Precautionary dissimulation (التقية) is my religion and the religion of my fathers; there is no religion for one who has no taqiyyah; and taqiyyah is the shield of Allah on earth." For had the Believer from the Family of Pharaoh manifested his Islam, he would have been killed.

I say: The narrations from the Shi‘i paths regarding this are numerous, and the verses support them, such as His saying: إِلَّا أَنْ تَتَّقُوا مِنْهُمْ تُقاةً — "unless you [do it] to safeguard yourselves against them" (3:28) and His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ — "excepting him who is forced while his heart is at rest in faith" (16:106).

In al-Mahasin, with its chain of narration from Ayyub ibn al-Hurr, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding the saying of Allah: فَوَقاهُ اللهُ سَيِّئاتِ ما مَكَرُوا — "So Allah saved him from the evils of their stratagems," he said: "As for them, they did indeed assault him and kill him—but do you know what He saved him from? He saved him from being tempted away from his religion."

I say: Some other narrations carry this meaning, while in some of what has been narrated through Sunni paths, it is said that Allah saved him from being killed.

In al-Khisal, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "I wonder at the one who is frightened by four things, how he does not flee to four others?"—until he said—"And I wonder at the one who is plotted against, how he does not flee to His saying: وَأُفَوِّضُ أَمْرِي إِلَى اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ بَصِيرٌ بِالْعِبادِ — 'and I entrust my affair to Allah. Indeed Allah is watchful of [His] servants,' for I heard Allah the Exalted say immediately following it: فَوَقاهُ اللهُ سَيِّئاتِ ما مَكَرُوا — 'So Allah saved him from the evils of their stratagems.'"

I say: This is narrated in books other than this one.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, a man said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "What do you say regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا — '[It is] the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening'?" Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "What do the people say?" He replied: "They say: It is in the Fire of eternity—and they are not punished in between those times." He said: "Then they are among the felicitous!" It was said to him: "May I be your ransom, how is this?" He said: "This is only in the world (الدنيا); as for the abode of eternity, it is His saying: يَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذابِ — 'and on the day when the Hour sets in [it will be said], “Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment.”'"

I say: His (peace be upon him) meaning by "the world" (الدنيا) is the intermediate realm (البرزخ), and this usage is frequent in their narrations.

In al-Majma‘, from Nafi‘, from Ibn ‘Umar, that the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) said: "إن أحدكم إذا مات عرض عليه مقعده بالغداة والعشي ـ فإن كان من أهل الجنة فمن الجنة ، وإن كان من أهل النار فمن النار ـ يقال : هذا مقعدك حتى يبعثك الله يوم القيامة — Indeed, when one of you dies, his seat is shown to him morning and evening—if he is of the people of Paradise, then from Paradise; and if he is of the people of the Fire, then from the Fire. It is said: 'This is your seat until Allah resurrects you on the Day of Resurrection.'" Al-Bukhari and Muslim recorded it in the Sahih.

I say: Al-Suyuti narrated it in al-Durr al-Manthur from them both, and from Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ibn Mardawayh.

This meaning is frequently mentioned in the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), and much of it has already passed in the discussion on the intermediate realm (البرزخ) in the first volume of the book and other places.

Ayahs

فَٱصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِبْكَـٰرِ (٥٥)
So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true. Plead [Allah] for forgiveness of your sin, and celebrate the praise of your Lord morning and evening. (55)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَـٰنٍ أَتَىٰهُمْ ۙ إِن فِى صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ مَّا هُم بِبَـٰلِغِيهِ ۚ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (٥٦)
Indeed those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that may have come to them—there is [an ambition for] greatness in their breasts, which they will never attain. So seek the protection of Allah; indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (56)
لَخَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٧)
The creation of the heavens and the earth is surely more prodigious than the creation of mankind, but most people do not know. (57)
وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَلَا ٱلْمُسِىٓءُ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٥٨)
The blind one and the seer are not equal, neither are the evildoers and those who have faith and do righteous deeds. Little is the admonition that you take! (58)
إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَـَٔاتِيَةٌ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٩)
Indeed the Hour is bound to come; there is no doubt in it. But most people do not believe. (59)
وَقَالَ رَبُّكُمُ ٱدْعُونِىٓ أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِى سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ (٦٠)
Your Lord has said, ‘Call Me, and I will hear you!’ Indeed those who are disdainful of My worship will enter hell in utter humiliation. (60)

(Commentary)

When He related the story of Moses and his being sent with the truth to Pharaoh and his people, and their disputing the signs of Allah with falsehood and their plotting therein, and His—the Exalted—support for His prophet and His nullification of their guile, and what their affair led to in terms of the failure of their efforts and an evil return—He branched off from that by commanding His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient. He alerted him that the promise of Allah regarding victory is true, and that the guile of his people, their disputing with falsehood, and their arrogance (الاستكبار) — the act of seeking greatness and refusing to submit to the truth — against accepting his call will be nullified and will return as a bane upon themselves. They cannot frustrate Allah, and the promised Hour will surely come, and they shall enter Hell abased.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ "So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true" until the end of the verse. This is a derivation from what preceded regarding the command to take heed in His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ "Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them?" (40:21), and what was mentioned after it of the story of Moses, the outcome of the affair of the arrogant who dispute with falsehood, and His—the Exalted—support for the truth and its people.

The meaning is: Since the matter is as such, then be patient with the harm of the polytheists and their disputing with falsehood; indeed, the promise of Allah is true and He will fulfill for you what He promised. What is intended by the promise is what occurs shortly before this in His saying: إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith" (40:51), regarding the promise of victory.

And His saying: وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ "and plead for [forgiveness of] your sin." This is a command to him to seek forgiveness (الاستغفار) — the act of asking Allah to cover and erase one's shortcomings — for what is considered a sin (الذنب) in relation to him, even if it is not a sin in the sense of disobeying a masterly command, due to his infallibility (العصمة) (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). A discussion regarding the meaning of sin and forgiveness has already preceded at the end of the sixth volume of this book.

There is another meaning for the sin attributed to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) which we shall point out in the commentary (تفسير) at the beginning of Surah al-Fath, if Allah—the Exalted—wills. It has also been said: What is intended by his sin (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is the sin of his community, for which he was granted intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah.

And His saying: وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكارِ "and celebrate the praise of your Lord morning and evening." That is, glorify Him, the Glorified, accompanied by His praise for His beautiful favors, continuously and successively with the succession of days, or in every morning and evening. His saying "morning and evening" according to the first meaning is a form of metonymy (الكناية).

It has also been said: What is intended by it are the Dawn (الصبح) and Afternoon (العصر) prayers, and that the verse is Medinan.

However, it is established from the narrations—including the reports of the Ascension (المعراج)—that the five daily prayers were all made obligatory in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة). Thus, if the two obligatory prayers were intended here, that would have been in Mecca before the rest of the five prayers were made obligatory.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِ اللهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطانٍ أَتاهُمْ إِنْ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ ما هُمْ بِبالِغِيهِ "Indeed those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that may have come to them—there is nothing in their breasts but an ambition which they will never attain" (40:56). This is a reinforcement of what preceded in the previous verse regarding the command to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient and the comforting of his soul by confirming the promise of victory. Its summary is that these disputers will not reach their desire and shall never reach it; so let not their disputing grieve you, and be at peace regarding them.

Thus, His saying: إِنْ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ "there is nothing in their breasts but an ambition [pride]" is a restriction of the cause necessitating their disputing to pride (الكبر). That is, their motive in this is not the seeking of truth or doubt regarding Our signs such that they desire the manifestation of truth, nor do they possess any proof or authority (سلطان) such that they desire to manifest it. Rather, that which is in their breasts and which calls them to dispute is pride, by which they intend to refute the manifest truth.

And His saying: ما هُمْ بِبالِغِيهِ "which they will never attain." The pronoun refers to "pride" (الكبر) in consideration of its consequence; for pride is the cause of disputing, and disputing is intended to nullify the truth and erase the true call. The meaning is: they shall not reach their objective and desire from the disputing they engage in due to their pride.

And His saying: فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ "So seek refuge in Allah." That is, seek refuge in Allah from them and the pride they possess, just as Moses sought refuge from every arrogant disputer when he said: وَقالَ مُوسى إِنِّي عُذْتُ بِرَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ الْحِسابِ "Moses said, ‘Indeed I seek the protection of my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Reckoning’" (40:27).

And His saying: إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ "Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing." That is, the All-hearing of the supplications of His servants, the All-seeing of their needs, and the One Who sees the state they are in, whether in hardship or ease.

His saying, the Exalted: لَخَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "The creation of the heavens and the earth is surely greater than the creation of mankind, but most people do not know" (40:57). The lam is for an oath (القسم). What is intended by the heavens and the earth is the entirety of the universe. The meaning of the verse, according to what the context provides, is that they shall not reach their desire and they cannot frustrate Allah; for Allah, Who was capable of creating the entirety of the universe and was not frustrated by it despite its magnitude, is not frustrated by a small part of it, which is the humans who are created and are easier for Him. However, most people are ignorant (جاهلون); they think in their ignorance that they can frustrate Allah through a dispute they wage or any guile they plot.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ "The blind and the seeing are not equal" (40:58) until the end. When He mentioned that most people do not know, He reinforced it by stating that they are not of the same type. For among them is the blind and the seeing, and they are not equal. He then conjoined to them "those who have faith and do righteous deeds" and "the evildoer." The first group are people of insight (بصيرة) through which they take heed, while the second are those whose hearts Allah has blinded, so they do not take heed.

And His saying: قَلِيلاً ما تَتَذَكَّرُونَ "Little is the admonition that you take." This is an address to the people for the purpose of rebuke, and this is the reason for the transition (الالتفات) from the third person to the second person.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ لَآتِيَةٌ لا رَيْبَ فِيها وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يؤْمِنُونَ "The Hour is bound to come: there is no doubt about it, but most people do not have faith" (40:59). Allah reminded them in this verse of the coming of the Hour, and in the following verse of His Lordly call to them to supplicate to Him and worship Him, just as the believer from the family of Pharaoh alerted them in the previous story to the coming of the Hour and that the call belongs to Allah, while their deities have no call in this world nor in the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ رَبُّكُمُ ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ "Your Lord has said, ‘Call Me, and I will hear you [respond to you]’" (40:60). This is a call from Him, the Exalted, to His servants to supplicate to Him, and a promise of response (الاستجابة). He has mentioned the call, the supplication (الدعاء), and the response in absolute terms. We have discussed the meaning of supplication and response extensively under His saying, the Exalted: أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ "I answer the solicitor’s invocation when he visits Me with his prayer" (2:186) in the first volume of this book.

And His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِي سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ داخِرِينَ "Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter hell, abased." الدخور (Abasement) means humiliation (الذلة). He has substituted "supplication" with "worship" (عبادة), which indicates that supplication is worship.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Sahifah al-Sajjadiyyah: وقلت : «ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْـإِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِيـسَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ داخِرِينَ» فسميت دعاءك عبادة وتركه استكبارا ـ وتوعدت على تركه دخول جهنم داخرين. — "And You said: 'Call Me, and I will respond to you; indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter Hell, abased.' Thus, You named Your supplication 'worship' and its abandonment 'arrogance,' and You threatened for its abandonment the entry into Hell, abased."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Hammad ibn 'Isa, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: سمعته يقول : ادع ولا تقل : قد فرغ من الأمر ـ فإن الدعاء هو العبادة إن الله عز وجل يقول : «إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِيـسَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ داخِرِينَ» وقال : «ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ». — "I heard him saying: 'Supplicate and do not say: the matter has already been decided. For indeed, supplication is worship; indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, says: "Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter Hell, abased," and He said: "Call Me, and I will respond to you."'"

I say: His saying (peace be upon him): "For indeed, supplication..." until "...abased" is an argument for what he first exhorted toward in his saying: "Supplicate." And his saying: "And He said: 'Call Me, and I will respond to you'" is an argument for what he said secondly: "And do not say: the matter has already been decided." This is why he (peace be upon him) presented the end of the verse over its beginning in his explanation.

In al-Khisal, from Mu'awiyah ibn 'Ammar, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: يا معاوية من أعطي ثلاثة لم يحرم ثلاثة : من أعطي الدعاء أعطي الإجابة ، ومن أعطي الشكر أعطي الزيادة ـ ومن أعطي التوكل أعطي الكفاية ـ فإن الله عز وجل يقول في كتابه : «وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ» وقال : «لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ» ، وقال : «ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ». — "O Mu'awiyah, whoever is given three things is not deprived of three: whoever is given [the success to] supplication is given the response; whoever is given [the success to] gratitude is given increase; and whoever is given [the success to] trust (التوكل) — the act of relying solely upon Allah — is given sufficiency. For Allah, Mighty and Majestic, says in His Book: 'And whoever trusts in Allah, He is sufficient for him' (65:3), and He said: 'If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing]' (14:7), and He said: 'Call Me, and I will respond to you' (40:60)."

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of narration to Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: قال قوم للصادق عليه‌السلام : ندعوه فلا يستجاب لنا. قال : لأنكم تدعون من لا تعرفونه. — "A group said to al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): 'We supplicate to Him but we are not answered.' He said: 'Because you supplicate to One Whom you do not know.'"

I say: We have already cited a number of narrations regarding supplication under His saying: أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ "I answer the solicitor’s invocation when he visits Me with his prayer" (2:186) in the first volume of this book.

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَٱلنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٦١)
It is Allah who made the night for you, that you may rest in it, and the day to provide visibility. Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most people do not give thanks. (61)
ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ (٦٢)
That is Allah, your Lord, the creator of all things, there is no god except Him. Then where do you stray? (62)
كَذَٰلِكَ يُؤْفَكُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ (٦٣)
Those who were used to impugning the signs of Allah are thus made to go astray. (63)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ قَرَارًا وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بِنَآءً وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ ۖ فَتَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦٤)
It is Allah who made for you the earth an abode and the sky a canopy. He formed you and perfected your forms, and provided you with all the good things. That is Allah, your Lord! Blessed is Allah, Lord of all the worlds! (64)
هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَٱدْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ ۗ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦٥)
He is the Living One, there is no god except Him. So supplicate Him, putting exclusive faith in Him. All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds. (65)
۞ قُلْ إِنِّى نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَمَّا جَآءَنِىَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ مِن رَّبِّى وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦٦)
Say, ‘I have been forbidden to worship those whom you invoke besides Allah, as manifest proofs have come to me from my Lord, and I have been commanded to submit to the Lord of all the worlds.’ (66)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ ثُمَّ يُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَشُدَّكُمْ ثُمَّ لِتَكُونُوا۟ شُيُوخًا ۚ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُتَوَفَّىٰ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَجَلًا مُّسَمًّى وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٧)
It is He who created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, then from a clinging mass, then He brings you forth as infants, then [He nurtures you] so that you may come of age, and then that you may become aged—though there are some of you who die earlier—and complete a specified term, and so that you may exercise your reason. (67)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ فَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٦٨)
It is He who gives life and brings death. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (68)

(Commentary)

The Glorified has returned a second time to point toward the signs of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — specifically the oneness of Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية), after having initially opened the discourse in this Surah with His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِهِ "It is He who shows you His signs" (40:13).

His saying, the Exalted: اللَّهُ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ وَالنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا It is Allah who made the night for you that you may rest in it, and the day to provide light. (40:61) to the end of the verse.

That is, He made the night dark for your sake so that you may rest therein from the exhaustion that afflicts you during the day due to striving for sustenance (الرزق), and He made the day "seeing" (مبصراً) so that you may seek of your Lord’s bounty and earn a living. This is among the pillars of the governance (تدبير) of human life.

It has thus become clear that attributing "sight" (الإبصار) to the day is a form of intellectual metaphor (المجاز العقلي), yet it contains no element of hyperbole (المبالغة) as some have claimed.

His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most people do not give thanks. (40:61) is an expression of favor (امتنان) upon them and a rebuke (تقريع) for their failure to show gratitude to Him in the face of this immense bounty. Had they been grateful, they would have worshipped Him. The word "mankind" (الناس) is repeated in place of a pronoun to indicate that it is the nature of human beings, qua human beings, to be ungrateful for blessings, as He said: إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ "Indeed man is most unfair and ungrateful" (14:34).

His saying, the Exalted: ذَلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ That is Allah, your Lord, the Creator of all things, there is no god except Him. So where do you stray? (40:62). That is, the One who governs the affairs of your life and your sustenance through the stillness of the night and the striving of the day is Allah, the Exalted; He is your Lord (الرب) because the governance (تدبير) of your affairs belongs to Him.

His saying: خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ "the Creator of all things" means He is the Lord of all things because He is the Creator of all things, and creation (الخلق) is inseparable from governance (التدبير). The necessary consequence of this is that there is no Lord in existence other than Him, neither for you nor for anyone else. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ "there is no god except Him," meaning there is no one truly worthy of worship (معبود بالحق) besides Him. For if there were another object of worship, there would be another Lord, as Divinity (الألوهية) is among the functions of Lordship (الربوبية).

His saying: فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ "So where do you stray?" means: how are you turned away from His worship toward the worship of others?

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَلِكَ يُؤْفَكُ الَّذِينَ كَانُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ Thus are those strayed who used to impugn the signs of Allah. (40:63). That is, in the same manner as this turning away, those who deny the signs of Allah are turned away. For the signs are manifest and not hidden; thus, turning away from what they indicate has no cause other than obstinate denial (الجحد).

His saying, the Exalted: اللَّهُ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ قَراراً وَالسَّماءَ بِناءً It is Allah who made the earth an abode for you, and the sky a canopy, (40:64) to the end of the verse. The "abode" (القرار) is the stable place upon which one settles. The "canopy" (البناء) — according to what has been said — refers to a dome (القبة), and from this comes the term used by the Arabs for the pitched tents (أبنية) under which they dwelt. The Exalted mentions the blessing of man’s stability upon the earth and beneath the sky.

His saying: وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ "and He formed you and perfected your forms." The particle fa (ف) here is for explanation (التفسير), meaning He perfected the creation of your forms. This is because man has been equipped with subtle intricacies in his form that enable him to perform diverse and wondrous actions that no other living being is capable of, and he enjoys the advantages of life in a way that is never possible for others.

His saying: وَرَزَقَكُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ "and provided you with good things." These are the diverse provisions that suit human nature by their very essence, such as grains, fruits, meats, and others. No animal possesses such diversity in its sustenance as man does.

His saying: ذَلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ "That is Allah, your Lord," meaning the Governor (المدبر) of your affairs. His saying: فَتَبارَكَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ "so blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" is praise for Him, Mighty and Majestic, for His Lordship over all creation. He branched this from His Lordship and governance of man to indicate that Lordship is one; His governance of human affairs is the very same as His governance of the affairs of all the worlds. For the prevailing system is a single system, and its application to each part is considered in its application to the whole. Thus, He, the Glorified, is the Source of Abundant Good (متبارك), the origin of much blessing; so blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين).

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الْحَيُّ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَادْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ He is the Living One, there is no god except Him. So supplicate Him, putting exclusive faith in Him. (40:65) etc. In the phrase هُوَ الْحَيُّ "He is the Living One," there is an absolute quality (إطلاق) that is restricted neither by reason (عقلاً) nor by revelation (نقلاً), in addition to the implication of exclusivity (الحصر). Its meaning is that to Allah, the Exalted, alone belongs a life (حياة) that is never permeated by death nor removed by annihilation (فناء). Thus, He, the Exalted, is Living by His Essence (بذاته), while everything else, whatever it may be, is living by His granting of life.

When there is One who is Living by His Essence and others who are living through another, none deserves worship by essence except the One who is Living by His Essence. For this reason, He followed His saying هُوَ الْحَيُّ "He is the Living One" with His saying لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ "there is no god except Him."

These two sentences were presented as a preamble to the command to supplicate Him — and not just any supplication, but to supplicate Him with monotheism and sincerity of religion (إخلاص الدين) for Him alone. This is because He is the Living by His Essence, unlike others, and because He is the One worthy of worship by essential right, unlike others. Therefore, He branched from His saying هُوَ الْحَيُّ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ His saying فَادْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ "So supplicate Him, putting exclusive faith in Him."

His saying: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds" is praise for Him for His Lordship over all creation.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنِّي نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ لَمَّا جَاءَنِيَ الْبَيِّنَاتُ مِنْ رَبِّي وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ Say, ‘I have been forbidden to worship those whom you invoke besides Allah, since manifest proofs have come to me from my Lord, and I have been commanded to submit to the Lord of all the worlds.’ (40:66). The meaning of the verse is clear. It contains a message of despair for the polytheists regarding the Prophet's agreement with them in worshipping their gods. This theme is repeated in Surah al-Zumar, and one might infer from this that this Surah was revealed after Surah al-Zumar.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ It is He who created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, (40:67) etc. What is meant by creating them from dust is the creation of their father, Adam, from dust, for the creation of everyone else traces back to him; thus, his creation from dust is their creation from it. Alternatively, it refers to the formation of the sperm-drop (النطفة) from the simple elements of the earth.

His saying: ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ "then from a drop of fluid" etc., means: then We created you from a lowly sperm-drop whose state is well-known; ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ "then from a clinging mass" likewise; ثُمَّ يُخْرِجُكُمْ "then He brings you forth" from the wombs of your mothers طِفْلاً "as infants" (أطفالاً). The word "infant" (الطفل) — as has been said — is used for both the singular and the plural. The Exalted said: أَوِ الطِّفْلِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلَى عَوْرَاتِ النِّسَاءِ "...or children who are not yet aware of women’s private parts" (24:31).

ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوا أَشُدَّكُمْ "then [He nurtures you] that you may come of age." The particle lam (ل) denotes the goal (للغاية), and its related verb is omitted; the estimation is: "then He brings you up so that you may reach your full strength," which is the period of life when faculties are at their peak. ثُمَّ لِتَكُونُوا شُيُوخاً "then that you may become old" is coordinated with "that you may come of age." وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُتَوَفَّى مِنْ قَبْلُ "and some of you are taken [by death] before" and thus do not reach these stages of life such as old age or full strength.

وَلِتَبْلُغُوا أَجَلاً مُسَمًّى "and that you may reach a specified term." This is the end of the appointed span which cannot be changed at all; it is a general goal for all people regardless of how long they live. The Exalted said: وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ "...and a term specified with Him" (6:2). For this reason, the sentence was not coordinated with "then" (ثُمَّ), so as to distinguish it from the two goals mentioned previously.

His saying: وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ "and so that you may exercise your reason," meaning that you may perceive the truth through the intellect (التعقل) ingrained within you. This is the goal of human creation in terms of his spiritual life (حياته المعنوية), just as reaching the specified term is the goal of his formal worldly life (حياته الدنيا الصورية).

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ It is He who gives life and brings death. (40:68) etc. That is, He is the One who performs the acts of giving life and bringing death. In these two acts is the transition of living beings from one world to another, and each of them is a starting point for His interventions through the blessings He bestows upon those whose affairs He governs.

His saying: فَإِذا قَضى أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (40:68). The explanation of this has been provided repeatedly.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated that ‘Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Abi Hatim reported with a sound chain from Abu al-‘Aliyah, who said: "The Jews came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'The Dajjal will be from us at the end of time,' and they spoke of his matter and magnified it, saying he would do such and such. So Allah revealed: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُجَادِلُونَ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَانٍ أَتَاهُمْ إِنْ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ مَا هُمْ بِبَالِغِيهِ 'Indeed those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that might have come to them—there is nothing in their breasts but a pride which they will never satisfy' (40:56). He said: 'He will not reach what he says.' Then He said: فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللَّهِ 'So seek refuge in Allah,' so He commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to seek refuge from the tribulation of the Dajjal. لَخَلْقُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ 'The creation of the heavens and the earth is surely greater than the creation of mankind' (40:57) — meaning the Dajjal."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ka‘b al-Ahbar regarding His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُجَادِلُونَ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَانٍ "Indeed those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority," he said: "They are the Jews; it was revealed concerning them regarding what they expect of the matter of the Dajjal."

In the same source, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Jurayj regarding His saying: لَخَلْقُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ "The creation of the heavens and the earth is surely greater than the creation of mankind." He said: "They claimed that the Jews said: 'There will be a king from us at the end of time; the sea will reach his knees, the clouds will be below his head, he will take birds from between the sky and the earth, and he will have a mountain of bread and a river.' So it was revealed: لَخَلْقُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ."

I say: You have already seen in what preceded that the purpose of the Surah — as inferred from the context of its verses — is to speak about their arrogance and their disputing the signs of Allah unjustly. The discourse begins with this and returns to it time and again, such as His saying: مَا يُجَادِلُ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا "None dispute the signs of Allah except the faithless" (40:4), and His saying: وَجَادَلُوا بِالْبَاطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا بِهِ الْحَقَّ "...and they disputed by means of falsehood to refute the truth thereby" (40:5), and His saying: الَّذِينَ يُجَادِلُونَ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَانٍ أَتَاهُمْ كَبُرَ مَقْتًا "Those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that might have come to them—[that is] greatly detestable..." (40:35), and His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُجَادِلُونَ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَانٍ أَتَاهُمْ إِنْ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ "Indeed those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that might have come to them—there is nothing in their breasts but a pride..." (40:56), and His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يُجَادِلُونَ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ أَنَّى يُصْرَفُونَ "Have you not regarded those who dispute the signs of Allah, how they are left to stray?" (40:69).

Thus, the context of the verses of the Surah refuses to allow some of them to be restricted to a specific occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) that the others do not share, as is the implication of these three narrations.
