Furthermore, the story mentioned in the narrations regarding the Jews' report of the Dajjal does not align clearly with the two verses after reflecting upon the content of the verses themselves — namely His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُجَادِلُونَ "Indeed those who dispute..." until His saying: وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ "...but most people do not know" (40:57).

From this, it becomes evident that the view claiming these two verses are Medinan, based on these narrations, is as you see [unfounded].

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ أَنَّىٰ يُصْرَفُونَ (٦٩)
Have you not regarded those who dispute the signs of Allah, where they are being led away [from Allah’s way]? (69)
ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَبِمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا بِهِۦ رُسُلَنَا ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٠)
—Those who deny the Book and what we have sent with Our apostles. Soon they will know (70)
إِذِ ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلُ فِىٓ أَعْنَـٰقِهِمْ وَٱلسَّلَـٰسِلُ يُسْحَبُونَ (٧١)
when they are dragged [with] iron collars and chains around their necks (71)
فِى ٱلْحَمِيمِ ثُمَّ فِى ٱلنَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ (٧٢)
into scalding waters and then set aflame in the Fire. (72)
ثُمَّ قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ (٧٣)
Then they will be told, ‘Where are they whom you used to worship as deities (73)
مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنَّا بَل لَّمْ نَكُن نَّدْعُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧٤)
besides Allah?’ They will say, ‘They have forsaken us. No, we did not invoke anything before.’ That is how Allah leads the faithless astray. (74)
ذَٰلِكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَمْرَحُونَ (٧٥)
‘That [punishment] is because you used to boast unduly on the earth and because you used to strut. (75)
ٱدْخُلُوٓا۟ أَبْوَٰبَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ (٧٦)
Enter the gates of hell, to remain in it [forever].’ Evil is the [final] abode of the arrogant. (76)
فَٱصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ ۚ فَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنَا يُرْجَعُونَ (٧٧)
So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true. Whether We show you a part of what We promise them, or take you away [before that], [in any case] they will be brought back to Us. (77)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًا مِّن قَبْلِكَ مِنْهُم مَّن قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ قُضِىَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ (٧٨)
Certainly We have sent apostles before you. Of them are those We have recounted to you, and of them are those We have not recounted to you. An apostle may not bring any sign except by Allah’s permission. Hence, when Allah’s edict comes, judgment is made with justice, and it is thence that the falsifiers become losers. (78)

(Commentary)

This is a return, once again, to the discourse regarding those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah (آيات الله). The surah has already addressed the ultimate fate of their affair by mentioning the end that befell their likes among the past nations, and Allah’s support for His religion at the beginning of the surah in general terms. It then detailed the circumstances of the call of Moses (عليه السلام) specifically, in the account narrated of his story and the specific victory Allah granted him, followed by the command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to be patient and the promise of victory.

This is the final turn against them, in which their ultimate end and that toward which they are being diverted—namely, eternal punishment—is mentioned. Then the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is commanded to be patient and promised victory, and his soul is comforted by the fact that the promise of Allah is true.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِ اللهِ أَنَّى يُصْرَفُونَ "Have you not regarded those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah, how they are left to stray?" (40:69). The phrase "Have you not regarded" (ألم تر) serves to express wonderment, and "how" (أنى) means "in what manner." The meaning is: Do you not wonder, or have you not wondered, at the affair of these who dispute concerning the signs of Allah—how they are diverted from the truth to falsehood, and from guidance to error?

The mention of the state of the disputers here is from the perspective of pointing out that they are diverted from the truth and guidance and the ultimate consequence of that. In contrast, the previous mention in His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِ اللهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطانٍ أَتاهُمْ إِنْ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ ما هُمْ بِبالِغِيهِ "Indeed those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah without any authority that might have come to them—there is nothing in their breasts but a pride which they will not satisfy" (40:56), was from the perspective that the motive driving them to this is pride (الكبر), and that they shall not attain what they desire. Thus, there is no repetition.

From this, the weakness in the view of some becomes apparent: that the repetition of the mention of disputation is based on the plurality of the disputers—such that the disputers mentioned in the previous verse are different from those mentioned in this verse—or based on the difference in the subject of the dispute, as if the dispute there was regarding the Resurrection and here regarding monotheism (التوحيد). Furthermore, such views overlook the overall purpose of the surah, as you have come to know.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِالْكِتابِ وَبِما أَرْسَلْنا بِهِ رُسُلَنا فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ "Those who deny the Book and what We have sent Our apostles with. Soon they will know" (40:70). What the context of the subsequent verses provides is that the intended meaning of these disputers is the disputers from among the people of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Accordingly, it is most appropriate that "the Book" (الكتاب) refers to the Noble Quran, and His saying "and what We have sent Our apostles with" (وبما أرسلنا به رسلنا) refers to what the messengers (عليه السلام) brought from Allah of scripture and religion (الدين), for the idolaters are deniers of prophethood.

His saying "Soon they will know" (فسوف يعلمون) is a consequence derived from their disputation and denial, and it is a threat to them; meaning, they will soon know the reality of their disputing the signs of Allah and their denial of the Book and the messengers.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذِ الْأَغْلالُ فِي أَعْناقِهِمْ وَالسَّلاسِلُ يُسْحَبُونَ فِي الْحَمِيمِ ثُمَّ فِي النَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ "When the iron collars will be around their necks and the chains, and they will be dragged into the boiling water, then they will be fueled into the Fire" (40:71-72). In al-Majma', it is stated: "The iron collars (الأغلال) is the plural of ghull, which is a collar placed around the neck for humiliation and pain, and its root meaning is 'entry'." He said: "The chains (السلاسل) is the plural of silsilah, which are rings arranged lengthwise in a continuous manner." He said: "Dragging (السحب) is to pull something along the ground; this is its root." He said: "Fueling (السجر), its root is to throw wood into the main part of a fire, like an oven (التنور) that is fueled with fuel." End of quote.

His saying "When the iron collars will be around their necks and the chains" (إذ الأغلال في أعناقهم والسلاسل) is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) for His saying "Soon they will know." It has been said that the use of "when" (إذ)—which is for the past—is to indicate the certainty of the occurrence (تحقق الوقوع), even if its timing is in the future; thus, there is no contradiction in combining "soon" (سوف) and "when" (إذ).

"The iron collars will be around their necks" (الأغلال في أعناقهم) is a subject and predicate, and "the chains" (السلاسل) is conjoined to "the iron collars." "They will be dragged into the boiling water" (يسحبون في الحميم) is a subsequent predicate, and "then they will be fueled into the Fire" (ثم في النار يسجرون) is conjoined to "they will be dragged."

The meaning is: They will soon know the reality of their deeds when the collars and chains are around their necks, being dragged through scalding water of intense heat, then cast into the Fire.

It has also been said that the meaning of His saying "then they will be fueled into the Fire" is that they will become the fuel of the Fire. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted, in describing Hell: وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَالْحِجارَةُ "whose fuel is humans and stones" (2:24), and His saying: إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ "Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell" (21:98).

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ قالُوا ضَلُّوا عَنَّا "Then they will be asked, ‘Where are those you used to ascribe as partners besides Allah?’ They will say, ‘They have forsaken us...’" (40:73-74) to the end of the verse. That is, it is said to them while they are alternating between being dragged and being fueled: Where are those you used to associate as partners besides Allah, so that they might help you by saving you from this punishment or intercede for you, as you used to claim they would intercede for you in exchange for your worship of them?

His saying "They will say, ‘They have forsaken us’" (قالوا ضلوا عنا) means they have disappeared from us; from the expression "the beast strayed" (ضلت الدابة) when it disappears and its location is unknown. This is their answer to what was said to them: "Where are those you used to ascribe as partners besides Allah?"

His saying "‘...rather, we did not use to invoke anything before’" (بل لم نكن ندعوا من قبل شيئاً) is a retraction (إضراب) from their first answer, because it becomes clear to them that the deities they used to claim as partners were nothing but names without named entities, and concepts to which nothing corresponded, and their worship of them was nothing but in vain. Therefore, they denied that they used to worship anything. The Exalted said: فَزَيَّلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ "We shall set them apart" (10:28), and He said: لَقَدْ تَقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْكُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ "Certainly the tie between you has been severed, and what you used to claim has forsaken you" (6:94).

It has also been said that this is part of their lying on the Day of Resurrection, similar to His saying: وَاللهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ "By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists" (6:23).

His saying "Thus does Allah lead the faithless astray" (كذلك يضل الله الكافرين). That is, the leading astray (إضلال) by the Exalted of the disbelievers (الكافرين)—who are the concealers of the truth—resembles this straying; which is that they see falsehood as truth and pursue it, then it becomes clear to them after the failure of their striving that it was nothing but falsehood in the form of truth, and a mirage with the appearance of reality.

The meaning according to the second view—namely, that their saying "rather, we did not use to invoke anything before" is a lie—is: Like this leading astray, Allah leads the disbelievers astray, such that their affair ends in lying where it does not benefit, despite their knowledge that it will not benefit.

This sentence has been explained with other interpretations that are close to what we have mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَبِما كُنْتُمْ تَمْرَحُونَ "That is because you used to exult on the earth unjustly and because you used to be swaggering" (40:75). Joy (الفرح) is absolute happiness, while exultation (المرح) is excess in it, which is blameworthy. Al-Raghib said: "Joy (الفرح) is the expansion of the chest with a fleeting pleasure, and this most often occurs in physical pleasures." He also said: "Exultation (المرح) is the intensity of joy and being expansive in it." End of quote.

His saying "That is because you used to..." (ذلكم بما كنتم) refers to the punishment they are in, and the "B" (ب) in "because you used to" (بما كنتم) denotes causality (السببية) or recompense (المقابلة).

The meaning is: That punishment you are in is because of your exulting on the earth without right in fleeting pleasures, and because of your excessive joy. This was due to their hearts being attached to the fleeting goods of the world and its adornment, and their enmity toward every truth that opposed their falsehood; thus, they would rejoice and exult in the revival of their falsehood and the killing and oppression of the truth.

It is stated in al-Majma': "He qualified joy (الفرح) [with 'unjustly'] and left exultation (المرح) absolute, because joy can sometimes be for a right cause and thus be praised, or for a false cause and thus be blamed, whereas exultation (المرح) is never except for falsehood." End of quote.

His saying, the Exalted: ادْخُلُوا أَبْوابَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ "Enter the gates of hell to remain in them [forever]. Evil is the abode of the arrogant!" (40:76). That is, enter its gates assigned to you, remaining therein forever; for evil is the station of those who are arrogant (المتكبرين) toward the truth—Hell. It has been mentioned previously that the gates of Hell are its levels (دركاتها).

His saying, the Exalted: فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌ "So be patient! Indeed Allah’s promise is true" (40:77). When He clarified the ultimate end of those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah—which is the Fire—and that Allah leads them astray because of their disbelief, He followed it with the command to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to be patient, justifying it by the fact that the promise of Allah is true.

His saying: فَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِي نَعِدُهُمْ "Whether We show you a part of what We promise them" (40:77), which is the punishment of this world, أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ "or take you away" (40:77) by death so that We do not show you that, فَإِلَيْنا يُرْجَعُونَ "to Us they will be brought back" (40:77), and they shall not escape Us, so We shall fulfill against them what We have promised.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلاً مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ قَصَصْنا عَلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ لَمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ "Certainly We have sent apostles before you. Of them are those We have recounted to you and of them are those We have not recounted to you..." (40:78) to the end. This is an explanation of the nature of the victory mentioned in the previous verse: that the sign of victory—which Allah’s sunnah (سنة الله) has proceeded to send down to judge between every messenger and his nation and to make the truth manifest over falsehood, as indicated by His saying: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ "There is an apostle for every nation; so when their apostle comes, judgement is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged" (10:47)—was not delegated to any of the messengers before you. Rather, whoever among them brought it, brought it by the permission of Allah, and your case is like theirs. Thus, it is possible that We permit you to bring it, so We show you some of what We promise them, and it is possible that We take you away so We do not show you; however, when the command of Allah comes, judgment is made between them with truth, and the followers of falsehood (المبطلون) are the losers there. This is what the context provides.

So His saying: "Certainly We have sent apostles before you. Of them are those We have recounted to you and of them are those We have not recounted to you" is intended to point out that what He will mention is a prevailing sunnah (سنة جارية) from the Exalted.

His saying: وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ "An apostle may not bring a sign except by Allah’s leave" (40:78). Although the "sign" (الآية) is more general than the miraculous sign given to a messenger to support his message, and the sign that grants victory to the truth and judges between the messenger and his nation—all of which are by Allah’s permission—the subject of the discourse, as we have understood from the context, is the second type: that which judges between the messenger and his nation.

His saying: فَإِذا جاءَ أَمْرُ اللهِ قُضِيَ بِالْحَقِّ وَخَسِرَ هُنالِكَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ "When Allah’s edict comes, judgement is made with justice, and it is then that the falsifiers are the losers" (40:78). That is, when the command of Allah for punishment comes, judgment is made with truth; thus the truth is made manifest and falsehood is vanished, and at that point, those who clung to falsehood lose—in their world by destruction, and in their hereafter by eternal punishment.

This verse has been used as evidence that there are messengers whose stories are not mentioned in the Quran. However, this verse is Meccan and does not indicate more than the non-mention of the stories of some messengers up until the time of its revelation in Mecca. It has been mentioned in Surah al-Nisa: وَرُسُلاً قَدْ قَصَصْناهُمْ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَرُسُلاً لَمْ نَقْصُصْهُمْ عَلَيْكَ "and apostles We have recounted to you before and apostles We have not recounted to you" (4:164), and no name of any messenger mentioned by name in the Quran was mentioned in the surahs revealed after Surah al-Nisa.

In al-Majma', it is narrated from Imam Ali (عليه السلام) that he said: "Allah sent a black prophet whose story He did not recount to us." It is also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Tabarani in al-Awsat and Ibn Mardawayh from him to the same effect.

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمَ لِتَرْكَبُوا۟ مِنْهَا وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٧٩)
It is Allah who created the cattle for you that you may ride some of them, and some of them you eat; (79)
وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ وَلِتَبْلُغُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا حَاجَةً فِى صُدُورِكُمْ وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ (٨٠)
and there are [numerous] uses in them for you, and that over them you may satisfy any need that is in your breasts, and you are carried on them and on ships. (80)
وَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ فَأَىَّ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ تُنكِرُونَ (٨١)
He shows you His signs. So which of the signs of Allah do you deny? (81)
أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَانُوٓا۟ أَكْثَرَ مِنْهُمْ وَأَشَدَّ قُوَّةً وَءَاثَارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٨٢)
Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? They were more numerous than them and were greater [than them] in power and with respect to the effects [they left] in the land. But what they used to earn did not avail them. (82)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَرِحُوا۟ بِمَا عِندَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٨٣)
When their apostles brought them manifest proofs, they boasted about the knowledge they possessed, but they were besieged by what they used to deride. (83)
فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا۟ بَأْسَنَا قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَحْدَهُۥ وَكَفَرْنَا بِمَا كُنَّا بِهِۦ مُشْرِكِينَ (٨٤)
Then, when they sighted Our punishment, they said, ‘We believe in Allah alone, and disavow what we used to take as His partners.’ (84)
فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنفَعُهُمْ إِيمَـٰنُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا۟ بَأْسَنَا ۖ سُنَّتَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى قَدْ خَلَتْ فِى عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨٥)
But their faith was of no benefit to them when they sighted Our punishment—Allah’s precedent, which has passed among His servants, and it is thence that the faithless will be losers. (85)

(Commentary)

This is a return, after a previous return, to the mention of some signs of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and a redirection of the people toward taking heed from the state of the past, perished nations and the ongoing custom (سنة) of Allah regarding them. This custom involved sending His messengers to them and then judging between the messengers and their people, leading to the perdition of the disbelievers among them; at this point, the Surah concludes.

His saying, the Exalted: اللَّهُ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْعَامَ لِتَرْكَبُوا مِنْهَا وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ "It is Allah who made the cattle for you then you may ride some of them, and some of them you eat" (40:79). The Glorified mentions the livestock (الأنعام) — referring to camels, cows, and sheep — among the things from which man derives benefit in his life and through which he manages his affairs. It has also been said that the intended meaning here is specifically camels.

Thus, His saying: "made the cattle for you" (جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْعَامَ), the "making" (الجعل) here refers to creation (الخلق) or subjugation (التسخير). The "lam" in "that you may ride" (لِتَرْكَبُوا) denotes purpose, and "from" (من) denotes partitivity (التبعيض). The meaning is: He created for your sake, or subjugated for you, the livestock, and the purpose of this making is that you ride some of them, such as certain camels, and from some of them, such as other camels, cows, and sheep, you eat.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَافِعُ "And there are [other] profits in them for you" (40:80), such as your benefiting from their milk, wool, fur, hair, hides, and other such things. His saying: وَلِتَبْلُغُوا عَلَيْهَا حَاجَةً فِي صُدُورِكُمْ "and that through them you may satisfy a need in your breasts" (40:80) means: and among the purposes of making them is that you may reach, while being upon them by way of riding, a need in your breasts, which is the movement from one place to another for various objectives.

His saying: وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى الْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ "and you are carried on them and on the ships" (40:80) is a metaphorical expression for traversing land and sea by means of livestock and ships.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِهِ فَأَيَّ آيَاتِ اللَّهِ تُنْكِرُونَ "He shows you His signs. So which of the signs of Allah do you deny?" (40:81). The meaning of His showing His signs has already been discussed in the commentary on the beginning of the Surah. It is as if the sentence—meaning His saying: "He shows you His signs" (وَيُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِهِ)—is not intended for its own sake, which would necessitate repetition, but rather as a preamble and a preparation for the rebuke contained in His saying: "So which of the signs of Allah do you deny?" (فَأَيَّ آيَاتِ اللَّهِ تُنْكِرُونَ). That is: which of these signs that Allah shows you clearly and demonstrably do you deny with a denial that paves the way for you to turn away from His monotheism?

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا "Have they not travelled through the land and seen..." (40:82) until the end of the verse, is a rebuke to them and a redirection of their sight toward what has occurred of the custom (سنة) of judgment and decree regarding the past nations. A similar verse appeared at the beginning of the Surah, where the purpose was to clarify to them that Allah seized each of them for their sins because their messengers used to come to them with clear proofs (البينات) but they disbelieved in them; hence, that verse concluded with His saying: "so Allah seized them for their sins" (فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ). The purpose here, however, is to clarify to them that what they earned did not avail them, nor did the knowledge they possessed and exulted in benefit them in averting the punishment of Allah, nor did their repentance and regret for what they had done.

The verse begins with the "fa" of derivation (فاء التفريع), as it says: "Have they not travelled..." (أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا), along with a transition (التفات) from direct address to the third person. It is as if the speech is derived from His saying: "So which of the signs of Allah do you deny?" (فَأَيَّ آيَاتِ اللَّهِ تُنْكِرُونَ). Thus, after censuring them and denouncing their denial of His signs, He turned away from them toward the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, indicating their fall from the status of being addressed, and said: If His signs are manifest and clear, accepting no denial—and among them are the signs speaking through the ruins of the predecessors—and they have indeed travelled through the land and witnessed them, then why did they not look into them so it would become clear to them that the predecessors, despite being greater than these people in quantity and quality, were not benefited by the knowledge and power they exulted in?

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جَاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ فَرِحُوا بِمَا عِنْدَهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ "When their apostles brought them manifest proofs, they exulted in the knowledge they possessed..." (40:83). The plural pronouns in the verse—of which there are seven—refer to those who were before them. The "knowledge they possessed" (بِمَا عِنْدَهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ) refers to what had settled in their hearts and occupied their souls regarding the adornment of the worldly life and the various methods of management to attain it and reach its pleasures. Allah, the Glorified, has counted that as "knowledge" for them and restricted their knowledge to it, as He says: يَعْلَمُونَ ظَاهِراً مِنَ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ عَنِ الْآخِرَةِ هُمْ غَافِلُونَ "They know just an outward aspect of the life of the world, but they are oblivious of the Hereafter" (30:7), and He says: فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنَا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَيَاةَ الدُّنْيَا ذَلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ "So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge" (53:29-30).

The meaning of their "exulting in the knowledge they possessed" is their intense admiration for the expertise and outward knowledge they had acquired, and their attraction to it, which led to their turning away from the true gnosis (المعارف الحقيقية) brought by their messengers, as well as their belittling and mocking of it. Therefore, He followed their exulting in the knowledge they possessed with His saying: وَحَاقَ بِهِمْ مَا كَانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِئُونَ "and they were besieged by what they used to deride" (40:83).

Regarding the meaning of His saying: "they exulted in the knowledge they possessed" (فَرِحُوا بِمَا عِنْدَهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ), there are other opinions:

Among them: that the "knowledge they possessed" refers to their corrupt beliefs and false opinions, and calling them "knowledge" is by way of sarcasm (التهكم); they used to exult in them and consequently despise the knowledge of the messengers. You are well aware that this is a characterization without evidence.

Among them: that the "knowledge" refers to the sciences of the Greek philosophers and the materialists (الدهريين); when they heard of revelation and the knowledge of prophecy, they belittled the knowledge of the prophets and boasted of what they possessed. This is like the previous one, in addition to the fact that it does not apply to any of the nations whose stories the Quran narrates, such as the people of Nuh, 'Ad, Thamud, the people of Ibrahim, the people of Lut, the people of Shu'ayb, and others.

Among them: that the original meaning is: "When their messengers came to them with clear proofs, they did not exult in the knowledge that came to them," so "they exulted in the ignorance they possessed" was put in its place, and then "ignorance" was replaced with "knowledge" sarcastically, resulting in: "they exulted in the knowledge they possessed." This view—besides its artificiality (التكلف) and distance from common understanding—is subject to the same objections as the first.

Among them: that the pronoun in "they exulted" (فَرِحُوا) refers to the disbelievers, while the pronoun in "they possessed" (عِنْدَهُمْ) refers to the messengers. The meaning would be: the disbelievers exulted in the knowledge the messengers possessed with the exultation of laughter and mockery. The flaw here is that it necessitates a discrepancy in the consistent pronouns, in addition to the fact that laughter and mockery are not called "exultation" (فرح) without a contextual indicator.

Among them: that both pronouns in "they exulted in what they possessed" (فَرِحُوا بِمَا عِنْدَهُمْ) refer to the messengers. The meaning would be: when the messengers came to them and witnessed their ignorance, their persistence in disbelief and denial, and knew the consequence of their affair, they exulted in the true knowledge (العلم الحق) they possessed and thanked Allah for that.

The flaw here is that the context of the verses is the strongest witness that they were set forth to describe the state of the disbelievers after the messengers brought them clear proofs, and how it resulted in the descent of punishment, and how faith did not benefit them after witnessing the might (البأس). What connection does this have to the messengers exulting in their true knowledge? Furthermore, this also necessitates a discrepancy in the pronouns.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنَا قَالُوا آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَحْدَهُ وَكَفَرْنَا بِمَا كُنَّا بِهِ مُشْرِكِينَ "Then, when they sighted Our punishment, they said, ‘We believe in Allah alone, and we disbelieve in what we used to ascribe as partners to Him’" (40:84). The might (البأس) is the severity of the punishment, and the rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنْفَعُهُمْ إِيمَانُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنَا "But their faith did not benefit them when they sighted Our punishment..." (40:85). This is because faith at that moment is not based on free choice (الاختيار). His saying: سُنَّتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ فِي عِبَادِهِ "[This is] Allah’s precedent which has passed among His servants" (40:85) means: Allah has established it as an ongoing custom (سنة) among His servants that no repentance is accepted after the sighting of the punishment. وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ الْكَافِرُونَ "and there the faithless were the losers" (40:85).
